message.content: <local-command-caveat>Caveat: The messages below were generated by the user while running local commands. DO NOT respond to these messages or otherwise consider them in your response unless the user explicitly asks you to.</local-command-caveat>
<local-command-stdout>Set model to [1mDefault (Opus 4.6 · Most capable for complex work)[22m with [1mhigh[22m effort</local-command-stdout>

set up steve yegge beads in this project using best practices etc.  also look at the kapow and shatter projects in /home/ketan/project to see if there are useful basics for setting up a project to steal.  this project is meant to be an API service and web app for storing personal thoughts and notes.  it should also accept URLs via a browser plugin like a link sharing service.  I probably will  want an MCP server for working with AI agents.  I will want Google Drive integration as much of my information is already stored there.  I want it to have strong authentication and a postgresql type of backend (possibly with pg_vector).  I will want to be able to dictate notes on my phones (android and iphone), which may require native apps (progressive apps may be acceptable if they work).   I may want integration with Android Auto and Carplay so I can dictate notes while driving.  I will want the webapp to have a query interface and a way to list objects.  I think a React/Typescript front end and a Go-based API server are the right basic technologies, but I'm open to alternatives.  I will want to deploy this on the open internet for accessibility worldwide.  I am currently the only planned user, but maybe it ends up being really good, and other people want to use it. given the sensitivity of some of this information, I will want robust access control.  I will also want a good authentication and authorization architecture for requests that come in via MCP, which could include real-time per-request and/or per-response authorizations.


this is inpired by: https://promptkit.natebjones.com/20260224_uq1_guide_main

I've also heard good things about the Obsidian software package, but I don't want to use that, just steal ideas.

content.thinking:
The user wants me to set up Steve Yegge's "beads" system in this project, and they have a comprehensive vision for a personal knowledge management system. Let me break this down:

1. First, I need to understand what "beads" is - it's Steve Yegge's issue tracking/project management approach
2. Look at existing projects (kapow and shatter) for useful patterns
3. The project "flotsam" is meant to be a comprehensive personal knowledge/notes system

Let me start by:
1. Exploring the existing project structure
2. Looking at kapow and shatter for patterns
3. Fetching the beads guide from the URL provided


4. Then planning the architecture

Before diving into implementation, I should gather information in parallel - checking the existing project structure, examining those reference projects, and pulling in the beads documentation so I can understand the full scope of what needs to be set up.

content.text: I'll gather information from multiple sources in parallel before planning.

1→# Shatter
     2→
     3→Automatic exploratory testing via concolic execution. Shatter analyzes your functions, discovers branches, and generates inputs that exercise every code path — no test authoring required.
     4→
     5→## Installation
     6→
     7→**Quick install** (Linux/macOS):
     8→
     9→```bash
    10→curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/user/shatter/main/install.sh | bash
    11→```
    12→
    13→**Cargo install**:
    14→
    15→```bash
    16→cargo install --git https://github.com/user/shatter shatter-cli
    17→```
    18→
    19→**Build from source**:
    20→
    21→```bash
    22→git clone https://github.com/user/shatter.git
    23→cd shatter
    24→cargo build --release
    25→# Binary is at target/release/shatter
    26→```
    27→
    28→## Quickstart
    29→
    30→```bash
    31→# 1. Install shatter (see above)
    32→
    33→# 2. Write a function
    34→cat > shipping.ts <<'EOF'
    35→export function calculateShipping(weight: number, country: string): number {
    36→  if (weight <= 0) throw new Error("Invalid weight");
    37→  if (country === "US") return weight < 5 ? 5.99 : 12.99;
    38→  return weight < 5 ? 15.99 : 29.99;
    39→}
    40→EOF
    41→
    42→# 3. Explore it
    43→shatter explore shipping.ts:calculateShipping
    44→```
    45→
    46→Shatter finds every branch and generates inputs that reach each one.
    47→
    48→## How It Works
    49→
    50→Shatter uses **concolic execution** — a hybrid of concrete and symbolic execution:
    51→
    52→1. **Analyze**: Parses your function and identifies branch points (if/else, switch, try/catch, ternary, etc.)
    53→2. **Explore**: Runs the function concretely while collecting symbolic path constraints. After each run, it negates a constraint and solves for a new input (via Z3) that takes a different path.
    54→3. **Report**: Groups discovered paths into behavior clusters and shows which inputs trigger each behavior.
    55→
    56→This approach combines the precision of real execution with the systematic coverage of symbolic analysis.
    57→
    58→## Supported Languages
    59→
    60→| Language   | Status |
    61→|------------|--------|
    62→| TypeScript | Supported (via Node.js frontend) |
    63→| Go         | Supported (via Go frontend) |
    64→
    65→## CLI Reference
    66→
    67→All commands accept targets in `<file>:<function>` format (e.g., `src/math.ts:add`) or `<file>` to target all functions. File extension determines the language frontend (`.ts` = TypeScript, `.go` = Go).
    68→
    69→**Global options:** `--log-level <LEVEL>` (error/warn/info/debug/trace), `-v` (debug), `-vv` (trace), `-q` (quiet), `--perf` (show timing stats).
    70→
    71→See [`SPEC.md`](SPEC.md) for the full behavioral specification.
    72→
    73→### `shatter explore`
    74→
    75→Explore functions to discover branches and generate test inputs.
    76→
    77→```
    78→shatter explore [OPTIONS] <TARGETS>...
    79→```
    80→
    81→| Flag | Default | Description |
    82→|------|---------|-------------|
    83→| `--max-iterations N` | 100 | Maximum exploration iterations per function |
    84→| `--timeout SECS` | 60 | Timeout in seconds for entire exploration |
    85→| `--analyze-only` | -- | Only analyze branches, skip exploration |
    86→| `--show-clusters` | -- | Display behavior clusters in output |
    87→| `--scope PATH` | -- | Path to a `shatter.scope.yaml` file |
    88→| `--cache-dir DIR` | `.shatter/cache/` | Directory for caching behavior maps |
    89→| `--no-cache` | -- | Disable behavior map caching |
    90→| `--request-timeout SECS` | 30 | Per-request timeout for frontend responses |
    91→| `--exec-timeout SECS` | 10 | Per-invocation timeout in the frontend |
    92→| `--build-timeout SECS` | 30 | Timeout for compiling instrumented code |
    93→| `--inputs PATH` | -- | Path to a candidate inputs JSON file |
    94→| `--config PATH` | -- | Path to `.shatter/config.yaml` |
    95→| `--spec` | -- | Output a behavioral specification (markdown) |
    96→| `--spec-json` | -- | Output the behavioral specification as JSON |
    97→| `--no-boundary-values` | -- | Disable built-in boundary values as seeds |
    98→| `--invariants` | -- | Enable Daikon-style invariant detection |
    99→
   100→```bash
   101→shatter explore src/shipping.ts:calculateShipping
   102→shatter explore --analyze-only src/shipping.ts
   103→shatter explore --spec --invariants src/math.ts:add
   104→```
   105→
   106→### `shatter scan`
   107→
   108→Scan multiple functions in dependency order, using behavior maps as mocks.
   109→
   110→```
   111→shatter scan [OPTIONS] <TARGETS>...
   112→```
   113→
   114→| Flag | Default | Description |
   115→|------|---------|-------------|
   116→| `--max-iterations N` | 100 | Maximum iterations per function |
   117→| `--timeout SECS` | 120 | Timeout for the entire scan |
   118→| `--analyze-only` | -- | Only analyze, skip exploration |
   119→| `--scope PATH` | -- | Path to a scope config YAML file |
   120→| `--cache-dir DIR` | `.shatter/cache/` | Behavior map cache directory |
   121→| `--no-cache` | -- | Disable caching |
   122→| `--request-timeout SECS` | 30 | Per-request frontend timeout |
   123→| `--exec-timeout SECS` | 10 | Per-invocation timeout |
   124→| `--build-timeout SECS` | 30 | Build timeout |
   125→| `--parallelism N` | auto | Number of parallel frontend subprocesses |
   126→| `--timeout-per-fn SECS` | 30 | Per-function timeout (skip on exceed) |
   127→| `--output-dir DIR` | `./shatter-report/` | Write reports to this directory |
   128→| `--report FORMAT` | json | Report format: json, markdown, or both |
   129→| `--progress-json` | -- | Emit structured JSON progress events |
   130→| `--emit-tests FRAMEWORK` | -- | Generate tests: jest, vitest, or gotest |
   131→| `--emit-tests-dir DIR` | -- | Output directory for emitted test files |
   132→
   133→```bash
   134→shatter scan src/shipping.ts src/utils.ts
   135→shatter scan --report both --output-dir ./reports src/
   136→```
   137→
   138→### `shatter export-tests`
   139→
   140→Export generated tests from behavior maps produced by exploration.
   141→
   142→```
   143→shatter export-tests [OPTIONS] <TARGETS>...
   144→```
   145→
   146→| Flag | Default | Description |
   147→|------|---------|-------------|
   148→| `--framework NAME` | jest | Test framework: jest, vitest, or gotest |
   149→| `--module-path PATH` | `.` | Module path for imports |
   150→| `-o, --output PATH` | stdout | Write output to a file |
   151→| `--max-iterations N` | 100 | Maximum iterations for exploration |
   152→| `--timeout SECS` | 60 | Exploration timeout |
   153→| `--scope PATH` | -- | Scope config YAML file |
   154→| `--request-timeout SECS` | 30 | Per-request frontend timeout |
   155→| `--exec-timeout SECS` | 10 | Per-invocation timeout |
   156→| `--build-timeout SECS` | 30 | Build timeout |
   157→
   158→```bash
   159→shatter export-tests --framework jest -o shipping.test.ts src/shipping.ts:calculateShipping
   160→shatter export-tests --framework gotest pkg/utils.go:Validate
   161→```
   162→
   163→### `shatter run`
   164→
   165→Discover, analyze, and explore an entire repository in one shot.
   166→
   167→```
   168→shatter run [OPTIONS] <PATH>
   169→```
   170→
   171→| Flag | Default | Description |
   172→|------|---------|-------------|
   173→| `--output-dir DIR` | -- | Write per-function reports to this directory |
   174→| `--max-iterations N` | 50 | Maximum iterations per function |
   175→| `--timeout SECS` | 300 | Overall timeout |
   176→| `--analyze-only` | -- | Only discover and analyze, skip exploration |
   177→| `--request-timeout SECS` | 30 | Per-request frontend timeout |
   178→| `--exec-timeout SECS` | 10 | Per-invocation timeout |
   179→| `--build-timeout SECS` | 30 | Build timeout |
   180→
   181→```bash
   182→shatter run .
   183→shatter run --analyze-only --output-dir ./reports ./my-project
   184→```
   185→
   186→### `shatter diff`
   187→
   188→Compare current behaviors against a previous snapshot to detect regressions.
   189→
   190→```
   191→shatter diff [OPTIONS] <SNAPSHOT> <CURRENT>
   192→```
   193→
   194→| Flag | Default | Description |
   195→|------|---------|-------------|
   196→| `--json` | -- | Output diff as JSON instead of text |
   197→
   198→Exit code 0 when all behaviors match, nonzero when regressions are found.
   199→
   200→```bash
   201→shatter diff baseline.json current.json
   202→shatter diff --json old.json new.json
   203→```
   204→
   205→### `shatter spec-diff`
   206→
   207→Compare two function specifications and report behavioral changes.
   208→
   209→```
   210→shatter spec-diff [OPTIONS] <OLD> <NEW>
   211→```
   212→
   213→| Flag | Default | Description |
   214→|------|---------|-------------|
   215→| `--json` | -- | Output diff as JSON instead of text |
   216→
   217→Accepts spec JSON files produced by `explore --spec-json`. Exit code 0 when specs are equivalent, nonzero when regressions are found.
   218→
   219→```bash
   220→shatter spec-diff old-spec.json new-spec.json
   221→```
   222→
   223→## Development
   224→
   225→### Prerequisites
   226→
   227→- Rust toolchain (via [rustup](https://rustup.rs/))
   228→- Node.js 22+
   229→- Go 1.24+
   230→- libclang (`sudo apt install libclang-dev` on Ubuntu/Debian)
   231→- [beads](https://github.com/steveyegge/beads) (`npm install -g @beads/bd`) — issue tracker
   232→
   233→Or use the **devcontainer** — open in VS Code with the Dev Containers extension and everything is pre-configured.
   234→
   235→### First-Time Setup
   236→
   237→After cloning, initialize the beads issue tracker:
   238→
   239→```bash
   240→bd init --prefix str --from-jsonl --quiet
   241→bd import -i .beads/issues.jsonl
   242→bd config set beads.role maintainer
   243→```
   244→
   245→This bootstraps the local database from the checked-in issue history and installs git hooks that keep it in sync. The devcontainer does this automatically.
   246→
   247→### Project Structure
   248→
   249→```
   250→shatter-core/     Rust core engine (library crate)
   251→shatter-cli/      Rust CLI binary
   252→shatter-ts/       TypeScript frontend (Node.js subprocess)
   253→shatter-go/       Go frontend (Go binary subprocess)
   254→examples/         Example target functions for testing
   255→demo/             Walkthrough scripts
   256→```
   257→
   258→### Build & Test
   259→
   260→```bash
   261→# Build
   262→cargo build
   263→
   264→# Quick test (during development)
   265→cargo test
   266→
   267→# Standard test (before committing)
   268→cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings
   269→
   270→# Full test (before merging — includes all frontends)
   271→cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings
   272→cd shatter-ts && npm test && cd ..
   273→cd shatter-go && go test ./... && cd ..
   274→```
   275→
   276→### Demo
   277→
   278→Run the interactive walkthrough to see Shatter in action:
   279→
   280→```bash
   281→./demo/walkthrough.sh          # Interactive (pauses between steps)
   282→./demo/walkthrough.sh --auto   # Continuous
   283→```

1→# Shatter v2
     2→
     3→Automatic exploratory testing via concolic execution. Rust core engine with language-specific frontends (TypeScript, Go, Rust) communicating via JSON-over-stdio protocol.
     4→
     5→See `PLAN.md` for the full architecture and implementation roadmap.
     6→
     7→## Prerequisites
     8→
     9→For local dev: Rust toolchain, Node.js 22+, Go 1.24+, libclang. Run `./scripts/configure-bindgen.sh` if Z3 build fails. Devcontainer includes everything.
    10→
    11→## Code Quality Standards
    12→
    13→- All Rust code must pass `cargo clippy` with no warnings
    14→- All TypeScript code must pass strict mode (`strict: true` in tsconfig)
    15→- All Go code must pass `go vet` and `golangci-lint`
    16→- No `unwrap()` in Rust library code — use `Result` and `?`
    17→- No `any` type in TypeScript — use proper typing
    18→- Dependencies flow in one direction: cli → core, frontends → protocol
    19→- Integration tests use known-answer functions with expected branches and triggering inputs
    20→- Frontend protocol handlers have round-trip tests (serialize → deserialize → verify)
    21→- Regression snapshots are checked into the repo and verified in CI
    22→- **Bug fixes require a reproduction test first** — write an automated test that demonstrates the bug (must fail), then fix the code and verify the test passes. Never attempt a fix without a failing test.
    23→- **No magic numbers or string literals** — define named constants for default values, timeouts, error codes, capability lists, and any value that appears in both production code and tests. Tests must reference the constant, not duplicate the literal. Each language has a canonical location for constants (see per-language conventions below).
    24→- **No hardcoded absolute paths** — never write a literal absolute path (e.g. `"/home/user/project/..."`) in code, tests, output artifacts, or documentation. Runtime-computed absolute paths are fine and often necessary — use `path.resolve(...)` (TS), `filepath.Abs` (Go), or `canonicalize()` / `env::current_dir` (Rust). The rule prohibits hardcoded paths that bake in machine-specific locations, not absolute paths in general.
    25→- **Parallel code paths must maintain parity** — when two functions process the same domain (e.g. `buildSymExpr` and `buildSymExprWithFlow`, random explorer and concolic orchestrator, CLI wiring for `--concolic` vs default), they must handle the same cases. When adding a capability to one path, check the other. When adding a new AST node type, CLI flag, or config field, grep for the parallel code path and update it too.
    26→- **Rust contracts (`#[requires]`, `#[ensures]`)** — use only where ALL THREE hold: (1) trust boundary (Z3 FFI, subprocess JSON, cross-collection indices), (2) type gap (can't encode as a type), (3) silent corruption (violation propagates as wrong results, not panics). Additionally, there must be a plausible bug the contract catches that proptest wouldn't. See `shatter-core/CLAUDE.md` for the full policy and qualifying sites.
    27→
    28→See `/rust-conventions`, `/ts-conventions`, `/go-conventions` skills for detailed per-language standards.
    29→
    30→### File Format Standards
    31→
    32→- **YAML** is the format of choice for structured data and configuration files (not TOML, JSON, or XML)
    33→- **Markdown** is the format of choice for formatted text (documentation, plans, notes, specs)
    34→
    35→### Inline Documentation
    36→
    37→Comments explain **why** or document **non-obvious contracts** — never restate what the code already says. If a name needs a comment, choose a better name first.
    38→
    39→**What to document:**
    40→- Public API contracts: preconditions, postconditions, error behavior, ownership semantics
    41→- Non-obvious design choices: why an algorithm was chosen, why a field exists, why ordering matters
    42→- Known-answer test fixtures: expected branches, triggering inputs, and edge cases (see `examples/go/04-nested-control-flow.go` for the model)
    43→- `#[allow(...)]` / `@ts-ignore`: always explain why the suppression is needed
    44→
    45→**What NOT to document:**
    46→- What the code does when the code already says it (`// returns the sum` above `fn sum()`)
    47→- Type information visible in the signature (`// takes a string` above `fn foo(s: string)`)
    48→- Existence of language constructs (`// uses a switch statement`, `// is a simple struct`)
    49→
    50→**The delete test:** If you can delete a comment and the code is equally clear, delete it.
    51→
    52→**Bad → Good examples (from this codebase):**
    53→
    54→```go
    55→// BAD: restates syntax
    56→// SwitchOnString uses a switch statement.
    57→func SwitchOnString(color string) int {
    58→
    59→// GOOD: documents the test contract
    60→// SwitchOnString — 4 branches: "red"→1, "green"→2, "blue"→3, default→0.
    61→// Analyzer should detect all four arms and the string-equality conditions.
    62→func SwitchOnString(color string) int {
    63→```
    64→
    65→```go
    66→// BAD: restates the type definition
    67→// Point is a simple struct.
    68→type Point struct {
    69→
    70→// GOOD: no comment (the struct is self-describing). Or if it's testdata:
    71→// Point — test fixture for struct-field access analysis.
    72→type Point struct {
    73→```
    74→
    75→```rust
    76→// BAD: restates the signature
    77→/// Returns boundary values for the given type.
    78→pub fn boundaries_for(ty: &ParamType) -> Vec<BoundaryValue> {
    79→
    80→// GOOD: documents the contract
    81→/// Returns boundary values applicable to `ty`, ordered by category
    82→/// (limits first, then zeroes, then special values like NaN/empty).
    83→pub fn boundaries_for(ty: &ParamType) -> Vec<BoundaryValue> {
    84→```
    85→
    86→### Test Tiers
    87→
    88→| Tier | Command | Use when |
    89→|---|---|---|
    90→| Quick | `cargo test` | During development |
    91→| Standard | `cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings` | Before committing |
    92→| Full | Standard + `cd shatter-ts && npm test` + `cd shatter-go && go test ./...` + `cd shatter-rust && cargo test` | Before merge or when touching protocol definitions |
    93→| E2E | Full + `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` | After changing solver, instrumentor, explorer, orchestrator, or string ops |
    94→| Walkthrough | `bash demo/walkthrough.sh --auto --delay 0` | After changing CLI output, protocol, frontend execution, or example files |
    95→
    96→**E2E gate**: The E2E concolic tests (`shatter-core/tests/e2e_concolic.rs`) run the real TS frontend subprocess through analyze → instrument → explore → Z3 solve. They are the **only tests that validate the full pipeline end-to-end**. Unit tests alone are insufficient — a module can pass all its own tests while being silently disconnected from the pipeline (see "Completion checklist" below). Run E2E tests after any change to:
    97→- Solver logic (`solver.rs`, `string-ops.yaml`, `build.rs`)
    98→- Instrumentor (`instrumentor.ts`, especially `buildSymExpr*` functions)
    99→- Explorer or orchestrator (`explorer.rs`, `orchestrator.rs`)
   100→- Protocol types that affect execute responses (`protocol.rs`, `protocol.ts`)
   101→- CLI wiring that passes config to explorers (`main.rs`)
   102→
   103→**Walkthrough gate**: The walkthrough exercises the full pipeline end-to-end (analyze, explore, scan, export, spec). Run it after any change to CLI commands, frontend handlers, protocol types, or example files. The walkthrough prints an **ERROR SUMMARY** at the end and exits with code 1 if any step produced errors — check this summary, investigate failures, and fix before merging. Known exceptions: `stale` exit code 1 is informational (means "some functions are stale"), and scan errors for `11-opaque-types.ts` and `12-external-deps.ts` are expected.
   104→
   105→### Formal Methods & Verification
   106→
   107→Four complementary tools, each with a distinct role. PBT is the workhorse; the others fill gaps PBT can't reach.
   108→
   109→| Tool | Role | When to use |
   110→|---|---|---|
   111→| **Property-based testing** | Invariant discovery, regression prevention | Any non-trivial public function with invariants |
   112→| **Native fuzzing** | Crash resistance at parsing boundaries | Code that deserializes untrusted input |
   113→| **Contracts** (`contracts` crate) | Runtime assertions at trust boundaries | Only where Rust's type system can't express the invariant (see below) |
   114→| **Kani model checking** (deferred — P4) | Exhaustive verification of critical algorithms | Highest-stakes properties only (solver correctness). Not yet in use. |
   115→
   116→#### Property-Based Testing (primary strategy)
   117→
   118→Every component uses PBT: **proptest** (Rust), **fast-check** (TypeScript), **rapid** (Go). PBT is not optional decoration — it is a primary testing strategy alongside unit tests and E2E tests.
   119→
   120→**When adding or modifying a public function**, add property tests that cover its core invariants:
   121→- **Roundtrip properties**: serialize → deserialize → equality (table stakes — always include for serializable types)
   122→- **Semantic invariants**: "output types match input types", "length is preserved", "ordering is maintained" — these catch real bugs that fixed examples miss
   123→- **Pipeline composition**: test functions composed together, not just in isolation. The solver bridge (constraints → solve → overlay) and the explore loop (execute → classify → worklist) are especially important.
   124→- **Negative properties**: malformed/adversarial input never causes panics
   125→
   126→**Shared generators**: reuse `test_arbitraries.rs` (Rust), `arbSymExpr`/`arbTypeInfo` (TS), `genTypeInfo` (Go). Don't reinvent type generators per test file.
   127→
   128→**Coverage target**: every module that handles untrusted input, crosses an FFI boundary, or maintains state should have PBT coverage of its core invariants — not just serialization.
   129→
   130→See sub-crate CLAUDE.md files for per-component PBT priorities (`shatter-core`, `shatter-ts`, `shatter-go`).
   131→
   132→#### Native Fuzzing
   133→
   134→- **Go**: `testing.F` in `*_fuzz_test.go` files — byte-level mutation for crash/panic discovery at parsing boundaries. Seed corpus from existing test fixtures.
   135→- **Rust**: `cargo-fuzz` for deserialization boundaries.
   136→- Add a fuzz target for any code that deserializes untrusted input (protocol messages, subprocess JSON).
   137→
   138→#### Contracts
   139→
   140→High bar. Use `#[requires]`/`#[ensures]` only where ALL THREE hold: (1) trust boundary, (2) type gap, (3) silent corruption. See `shatter-core/CLAUDE.md` for the full policy, qualifying sites, and what does NOT qualify.
   141→
   142→#### Anti-Patterns
   143→
   144→- Contracts that restate type signatures — use the type system instead
   145→- Proptest for trivial getters/setters — specific examples are clearer
   146→- PBT that only tests serialization roundtrips without semantic invariants — roundtrips are table stakes, not the goal
   147→- Duplicating generators across test files — use shared generators in `test_arbitraries.rs` / `arbSymExpr` / `genTypeInfo`
   148→
   149→### Completion Checklist
   150→
   151→Before declaring any feature or bug fix **done**, verify:
   152→
   153→1. **Unit tests pass** — the module's own tests (Quick tier)
   154→2. **Clippy clean** — no warnings in the changed crate (Standard tier)
   155→3. **Property tests adequate** — if adding/modifying a public function, include proptest/fast-check/rapid properties covering its core invariants (not just serialization roundtrips)
   156→4. **Cross-language tests pass** — if touching protocol types (Full tier)
   157→5. **E2E pipeline works** — if touching any component in the analyze → instrument → execute → solve chain, run `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` and verify the pipeline still discovers expected branches
   158→6. **Walkthrough passes** — if touching CLI output or example files
   159→
   160→**Why this matters:** This project has multiple code paths that process the same data (random explorer vs. concolic orchestrator, `buildSymExpr` vs. `buildSymExprWithFlow`, CLI wiring for different explorer modes). Features that work on one path are routinely broken on others. The E2E tests are the only reliable way to catch cross-path regressions.
   161→
   162→**A feature is not done until it works end-to-end.** Closing an issue based on unit tests alone has repeatedly led to silent pipeline breakages that compound over time. If the E2E tests don't cover your change, add a new E2E test case before closing.
   163→
   164→## What NOT to Do
   165→
   166→- **Never edit generated protocol bindings** manually — regenerate from the schema
   167→- **Never commit** `.env` or files containing secrets — only `.env.example`
   168→- **Never add** `node_modules/`, `dist/`, or `target/` to git
   169→- **Never bypass clippy warnings** with `#[allow(...)]` without a comment explaining why
   170→- **Never add a CLI command** without updating `demo/walkthrough.sh`
   171→- **Never close a pipeline feature based on unit tests alone** — run `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` to verify end-to-end behavior. Unit tests prove a module works in isolation; E2E tests prove it works in the pipeline. Both are required.
   172→- **Never hardcode absolute paths** — no literal paths like `"/home/user/..."` or `"/tmp/specific-dir/..."` in source, tests, or config. Runtime-computed absolute paths (from `canonicalize()`, `current_dir()`, `path.resolve()`, etc.) are fine.
   173→- **Never add a capability to one explorer path without checking the other** — the random explorer (`explorer.rs`) and concolic orchestrator (`orchestrator.rs`) are wired differently in `main.rs`. A feature added to one is routinely missing from the other (see str-emw6). Grep for the parallel path before declaring done.
   174→
   175→## Common Task Recipes
   176→
   177→### Add a new protocol message type
   178→
   179→1. Define the message in `shatter-core/src/protocol.rs` (Rust types + serde)
   180→2. Add round-trip serialization tests in the same module
   181→3. Implement the handler in each frontend:
   182→   - TypeScript: `shatter-ts/src/protocol.ts`
   183→   - Go: `shatter-go/protocol/`
   184→   - Rust: `shatter-rust/src/protocol.rs`
   185→4. Add round-trip tests in each frontend (serialize → deserialize → verify)
   186→
   187→### Add a new CLI command
   188→
   189→1. Add the clap subcommand in `shatter-cli/src/`
   190→2. Implement the handler, delegating to `shatter-core` for logic
   191→3. Add integration tests exercising the command
   192→4. Update `demo/walkthrough.sh` to exercise the new command
   193→
   194→### Add an integration test with known-answer functions
   195→
   196→1. Write the target function in `examples/typescript/src/` (or the relevant language)
   197→2. Document the expected branches and triggering inputs in a comment
   198→3. Write the test in `shatter-core` that invokes the engine and asserts all branches are found
   199→4. Check in a regression snapshot of the output
   200→
   201→### Frontend timeout contract
   202→
   203→All frontends MUST read the `SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT` env var (seconds) and apply it to function execution. The CLI sets this var from `--timeout` before spawning frontends.
   204→
   205→| Frontend | Default | Env var | Implementation |
   206→|---|---|---|---|
   207→| Go | 5s | `SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT` | `execTimeout()` in `instrument/executor.go` |
   208→| TypeScript | 15s | `SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT` | `getExecTimeoutMs()` in `src/executor.ts` |
   209→| Rust | 5s | `SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT` | `exec_timeout_from_env()` in `src/handler.rs` (stored, not yet applied — execute is unimplemented) |
   210→
   211→Invalid values (non-numeric, zero, negative) fall back to the default silently.
   212→
   213→## Output Review
   214→
   215→After any change affecting CLI output, frontend logging, or protocol formatting, run `/walkthrough-review` to validate the output is human-readable.
   216→
   217→Update README.md when build/run/config procedures change.
   218→
   219→## Agent Workflow
   220→
   221→See `AGENTS.md` for issue tracking (beads), git workflow, and agent operational instructions.
   222→
   223→### Sprint Workflow
   224→
   225→When asked to work on ready issues in parallel, **invoke `/swarm`** (the global skill handles team/worktree/merge mechanics). For epic-based work or Shatter-specific quality gates, also invoke `/swarm-project` which adds wave scheduling via `bd swarm` and runs `/check-all` + `/walkthrough-review`.
   226→
   227→### Research Memory
   228→
   229→After researching codebase architecture or feature implementation status, save factual findings to project memory proactively — don't wait for the user to ask. Tag entries with date so stale facts can be identified later. This applies to any confirmed facts learned by reading code: what's implemented vs stubbed, how mechanisms work, which frontends support which features, etc.
   230→
   231→### Plans
   232→
   233→When a planning session produces a plan worth preserving, copy it from `~/.claude/plans/` into `docs/plans/` with a filename including the issue key and a descriptive name (e.g., `str-kapl-resilience-timeouts-memory.md`). Reference the plan from the relevant beads issue(s) via `--notes`.
   234→
   235→### Efficiency Rules
   236→
   237→- **Batch `bd show` calls**: `bd show X && echo --- && bd show Y && echo --- && bd show Z` — never sequential individual calls.
   238→- **Before `git merge`**: Always run `git branch --show-current` to verify you are on main. If not, `git checkout main` first.
   239→- **After context compaction**: Trust the summary. Do not re-run git status, git diff, git log, or test suites that the pre-compaction portion already completed.
   240→- **AskUserQuestion**: Only for decisions where the wrong choice requires significant rework. For preference questions, pick a sensible default and proceed.
   241→

   243→@shatter-cli/CLAUDE.md
   244→@shatter-ts/CLAUDE.md
   245→@shatter-go/CLAUDE.md
   246→@shatter-rust/CLAUDE.md

1→.PHONY: help build build-rust build-core build-cli build-rust-frontend build-examples build-ts build-go \
     2→       test test-quick test-standard test-full test-e2e \
     3→       clean lint walkthrough
     4→
     5→# -- Build -------------------------------------------------------------------
     6→
     7→build: build-rust build-ts build-go ## Build everything (Rust + TS + Go)
     8→
     9→help: ## Show this help
    10→	@grep -E '^[a-zA-Z_-]+:.*?## ' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) | \
    11→		awk 'BEGIN {FS = ":.*?## "}; {printf "  \033[36m%-18s\033[0m %s\n", $$1, $$2}'
    12→
    13→build-rust: build-core build-cli build-rust-frontend build-examples ## Build all Rust crates
    14→
    15→build-core: ## Build shatter-core library
    16→	cargo build -p shatter-core
    17→
    18→build-cli: ## Build shatter CLI binary
    19→	cargo build -p shatter-cli
    20→
    21→build-rust-frontend: ## Build Rust frontend + runtime
    22→	cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust/Cargo.toml
    23→	cargo build --manifest-path shatter-rust-runtime/Cargo.toml
    24→
    25→build-examples: ## Build example targets (Rust + WASM generators)
    26→	cargo build --manifest-path examples/rust/Cargo.toml
    27→	cargo build --manifest-path examples/generators/wasm-rust/Cargo.toml
    28→	cargo build --manifest-path examples/generators/wasm-adversarial/Cargo.toml
    29→
    30→build-ts: ## Build TypeScript frontend
    31→	cd shatter-ts && npm install --silent && npm run build
    32→
    33→build-go: ## Build Go frontend
    34→	cd shatter-go && go build ./...
    35→
    36→# -- Test tiers (see CLAUDE.md) -----------------------------------------------
    37→
    38→test: test-full ## Run full test suite (default)
    39→
    40→test-quick: build ## Quick: cargo test only
    41→	cargo test
    42→
    43→test-standard: build ## Standard: cargo test + clippy
    44→	cargo test
    45→	cargo clippy -- -D warnings
    46→
    47→test-full: build ## Full: all languages + clippy
    48→	cargo test
    49→	cargo clippy -- -D warnings
    50→	cd shatter-ts && npm test
    51→	cd shatter-go && go test ./...
    52→
    53→test-e2e: build ## E2E: concolic pipeline tests only
    54→	cargo test --test e2e_concolic
    55→
    56→# -- Other --------------------------------------------------------------------
    57→
    58→lint: ## Run linters (clippy + golangci-lint)
    59→	cargo clippy -- -D warnings
    60→	cd shatter-go && golangci-lint run ./...
    61→
    62→walkthrough: build ## Run the demo walkthrough
    63→	bash demo/walkthrough.sh --auto --delay 0
    64→
    65→clean: ## Remove all build artifacts
    66→	cargo clean
    67→	rm -rf shatter-ts/dist shatter-ts/node_modules
    68→	cd shatter-go && go clean ./...
    69→

1→# Beads - AI-Native Issue Tracking
     2→
     3→Welcome to Beads! This repository uses **Beads** for issue tracking - a modern, AI-native tool designed to live directly in your codebase alongside your code.
     4→
     5→## What is Beads?
     6→
     7→Beads is issue tracking that lives in your repo, making it perfect for AI coding agents and developers who want their issues close to their code. No web UI required - everything works through the CLI and integrates seamlessly with git.
     8→
     9→**Learn more:** [github.com/steveyegge/beads](https://github.com/steveyegge/beads)
    10→
    11→## Quick Start
    12→
    13→### Essential Commands
    14→
    15→```bash
    16→# Create new issues
    17→bd create "Add user authentication"
    18→
    19→# View all issues
    20→bd list
    21→
    22→# View issue details
    23→bd show <issue-id>
    24→
    25→# Update issue status
    26→bd update <issue-id> --status in_progress
    27→bd update <issue-id> --status done
    28→
    29→# Sync with Dolt remote
    30→bd dolt push
    31→```
    32→
    33→### Working with Issues
    34→
    35→Issues in Beads are:
    36→- **Git-native**: Stored in `.beads/issues.jsonl` and synced like code
    37→- **AI-friendly**: CLI-first design works perfectly with AI coding agents
    38→- **Branch-aware**: Issues can follow your branch workflow
    39→- **Always in sync**: Auto-syncs with your commits
    40→
    41→## Why Beads?
    42→
    43→✨ **AI-Native Design**
    44→- Built specifically for AI-assisted development workflows
    45→- CLI-first interface works seamlessly with AI coding agents
    46→- No context switching to web UIs
    47→
    48→🚀 **Developer Focused**
    49→- Issues live in your repo, right next to your code
    50→- Works offline, syncs when you push
    51→- Fast, lightweight, and stays out of your way
    52→
    53→🔧 **Git Integration**
    54→- Automatic sync with git commits
    55→- Branch-aware issue tracking
    56→- Intelligent JSONL merge resolution
    57→
    58→## Get Started with Beads
    59→
    60→Try Beads in your own projects:
    61→
    62→```bash
    63→# Install Beads
    64→curl -sSL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/steveyegge/beads/main/scripts/install.sh | bash
    65→
    66→# Initialize in your repo
    67→bd init
    68→
    69→# Create your first issue
    70→bd create "Try out Beads"
    71→```
    72→
    73→## Learn More
    74→
    75→- **Documentation**: [github.com/steveyegge/beads/docs](https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/tree/main/docs)
    76→- **Quick Start Guide**: Run `bd quickstart`
    77→- **Examples**: [github.com/steveyegge/beads/examples](https://github.com/steveyegge/beads/tree/main/examples)
    78→
    79→---
    80→
    81→*Beads: Issue tracking that moves at the speed of thought* ⚡
    82→

1→# Kapow — College Power Search
     2→
     3→A college/university search platform with a Go API backend and React frontend.
     4→
     5→## Quick Start with Docker (recommended)
     6→
     7→The fastest way to try Kapow — a single Docker image bundles PostgreSQL, the API
     8→server, and the web frontend with pre-loaded fixture data.
     9→
    10→```bash
    11→# Build the all-in-one image
    12→make test-image-build
    13→
    14→# Run it (exposes port 8080)
    15→make test-image-run
    16→```
    17→
    18→Open **http://localhost:8080** in your browser. To run the interactive demo
    19→against it, see [DEMO.md](DEMO.md).
    20→
    21→The container includes:
    22→
    23→- PostgreSQL 15 + PostGIS with pre-loaded college data
    24→- Go API server on internal port 8081
    25→- nginx reverse proxy serving the React frontend and proxying API requests
    26→
    27→No configuration needed — the container handles everything on first boot,
    28→including database initialization and fixture data loading.
    29→
    30→To stop: press `Ctrl+C`.
    31→
    32→---
    33→
    34→## Devcontainer
    35→
    36→See [.devcontainer/README.md](.devcontainer/README.md) for setup instructions
    37→(GitHub Codespaces, VS Code, and CLI).
    38→
    39→---
    40→
    41→## Local Development Setup
    42→
    43→### Prerequisites
    44→
    45→- **Go** 1.23+
    46→- **Node.js** 22+ with **pnpm** 9+ (`npm install -g pnpm`)
    47→- **Docker** (for the dev database container)
    48→- **psql** (PostgreSQL client) — `brew install libpq` on macOS, `apt install postgresql-client` on Linux
    49→
    50→Optional:
    51→
    52→- **golangci-lint** for linting
    53→- **air** for standalone API hot-reload: `go install github.com/air-verse/air@latest` (`make dev` uses `go run` instead, no install needed)
    54→
    55→### Quick start (recommended)
    56→
    57→A single command starts everything — PostgreSQL in Docker, the Go API with hot
    58→reload, and the Vite dev server:
    59→
    60→```bash
    61→make dev
    62→```
    63→
    64→This will:
    65→- Start a PostgreSQL + PostGIS container on port **15432** (won't conflict with a host PostgreSQL)
    66→- Load fixture data on first run (subsequent runs skip this step)
    67→- Run database migrations
    68→- Start the API with **air** (hot reload) on **:8081**
    69→- Start the web dev server on **:8080** with HMR
    70→
    71→Open **http://localhost:8080** and you're ready to go. Press **Ctrl+C** to stop
    72→everything. The database volume persists between runs, so the next `make dev`
    73→starts much faster.
    74→
    75→```bash
    76→make dev-clean    # remove this checkout's DB volume (forces fresh fixture load)
    77→make dev-prune    # remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days
    78→```
    79→
    80→### Manual setup (alternative)
    81→
    82→If you prefer to manage PostgreSQL yourself:
    83→
    84→#### 1. Configure environment
    85→
    86→```bash
    87→cp .env.example .env
    88→```
    89→
    90→Edit `.env` and set at minimum:
    91→
    92→```
    93→DATABASE_URL=postgres://user:password@localhost:5432/kapow?sslmode=disable
    94→JWT_SECRET=change-me-to-a-long-random-secret
    95→```
    96→
    97→#### 2. Set up the database
    98→
    99→Create the database, then run migrations:
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→createdb kapow
   103→make api-migrate-up
   104→```
   105→
   106→To load fixture data (from `common/src/main/resources/data/`):
   107→
   108→```bash
   109→xz -dc common/src/main/resources/data/complete.sql.xz | psql kapow
   110→```
   111→
   112→Smaller subsets are available for faster loading:
   113→
   114→- `subset-VT.sql.xz` — Vermont only
   115→- `subset-DE_MA_VT.sql.xz` — Delaware, Massachusetts, Vermont
   116→
   117→#### 3. Start the API
   118→
   119→```bash
   120→make api-run
   121→```
   122→
   123→The API listens on **localhost:8081**. Verify with:
   124→
   125→```bash
   126→curl http://localhost:8081/private/healthcheck | jq .
   127→```
   128→
   129→#### 4. Start the web frontend
   130→
   131→In a separate terminal:
   132→
   133→```bash
   134→make web-install   # first time only
   135→make web-dev
   136→```
   137→
   138→The dev server runs on **localhost:8080** and proxies `/graphql` and `/api`
   139→requests to the API at :8081.
   140→
   141→Open **http://localhost:8080**.
   142→
   143→#### 5. Authentication with Supabase (optional)
   144→
   145→The GraphQL endpoint (`/graphql`) requires a valid JWT. In development you can
   146→use any HS256 secret via `JWT_SECRET`. For production (or to test real login
   147→flows), connect a Supabase project.
   148→
   149→##### Create a Supabase project
   150→
   151→1. Go to [supabase.com/dashboard](https://supabase.com/dashboard) and create a
   152→   new project (or use an existing one).
   153→2. Note your **Project URL** (`https://<project-ref>.supabase.co`) and
   154→   **anon public** key from **Settings → API**.
   155→
   156→##### Configure API environment variables
   157→
   158→In `.env`, set the JWT vars so the Go API can validate Supabase-issued tokens:
   159→
   160→```
   161→JWT_SECRET=<your-supabase-jwt-secret>
   162→JWT_ISSUER=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1
   163→JWT_AUDIENCE=authenticated
   164→```
   165→
   166→The JWT Secret is on **Settings → API → JWT Secret**. Alternatively, paste the
   167→**JWT Public Key** (RS256) into `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` for asymmetric validation.
   168→
   169→##### Configure frontend environment variables
   170→
   171→The web frontend uses the Supabase JS SDK for login/signup. Add these to `.env`:
   172→
   173→```
   174→VITE_SUPABASE_URL=https://<project-ref>.supabase.co
   175→VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=<your-anon-key>
   176→```
   177→
   178→The `VITE_` prefix is required so Vite exposes them to browser code.
   179→
   180→##### Enable OAuth providers
   181→
   182→To support social login (Google, Apple, etc.):
   183→
   184→1. In the Supabase dashboard, go to **Authentication → Providers**.
   185→2. **Google**: Enable the Google provider, paste your Google OAuth **Client ID**
   186→   and **Client Secret** (from [Google Cloud Console → Credentials](https://console.cloud.google.com/apis/credentials)),
   187→   and add your Supabase callback URL as an authorized redirect URI in Google.
   188→3. **Apple**: Enable the Apple provider, upload your **Services ID**, **Team ID**,
   189→   **Key ID**, and **private key** (from
   190→   [Apple Developer → Certificates, Identifiers & Profiles](https://developer.apple.com/account/resources)).
   191→   Add the Supabase callback URL to your Apple Sign In configuration.
   192→
   193→Each provider's callback URL is shown in the Supabase dashboard when you enable
   194→it (typically `https://<project-ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1/callback`).
   195→
   196→##### How it works
   197→
   198→On the first authenticated request, the API automatically creates a personal
   199→team and membership for the user (using the JWT `sub` claim as the user ID).
   200→
   201→---
   202→
   203→## Project Structure
   204→
   205→```
   206→kapow/
   207→├── api/          # Go API server (GraphQL, search engine, auth)
   208→├── web/          # React + TypeScript + Vite frontend
   209→├── common/       # Shared resources (generated schema, fixture data)
   210→├── docker/       # Container config (nginx, supervisord, entrypoint)
   211→├── scripts/      # Shell utilities (smoke tests, schema generation)
   212→├── tools/        # Developer utilities (see below)
   213→├── Dockerfile    # All-in-one multi-stage build
   214→└── Makefile      # Root — delegates to api/ and web/
   215→```
   216→
   217→### tools/
   218→
   219→Developer utilities that operate on the database or fixture data.
   220→
   221→| Tool | Description |
   222→|---|---|
   223→| `tools/samplesql` | Deterministic institution sampler — connects to a live database, randomly selects N institutions (default 100, seed 42), extracts all dependent rows, and writes a self-contained SQL fixture file using `COPY` statements. Useful for generating small, reproducible test datasets. |
   224→
   225→**samplesql usage:**
   226→
   227→```bash
   228→cd tools/samplesql
   229→go run . --database-url "$DATABASE_URL" --limit 100 --seed 42 --output sample.sql
   230→```
   231→
   232→Flags: `--database-url` (or `DATABASE_URL` env var), `--limit` (number of institutions), `--seed` (deterministic random seed), `--output` (file path; defaults to stdout).
   233→
   234→---
   235→
   236→## Useful Commands
   237→
   238→### Development
   239→
   240→| Command | Description |
   241→|---|---|
   242→| `make dev` | Start full stack (DB + API + web) with hot reload |
   243→| `make dev-clean` | Remove dev DB volume (fresh fixture load on next run) |
   244→| `make dev-prune` | Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days |
   245→
   246→### API
   247→
   248→| Command | Description |
   249→|---|---|
   250→| `make api-run` | Start the API server |
   251→| `make api-test` | Run all Go tests (with race detector) |
   252→| `make api-test-unit` | Unit tests only (no DB required) |
   253→| `make api-generate` | Regenerate GraphQL code after schema changes |
   254→| `make api-migrate-up` | Apply pending database migrations |
   255→| `make api-migrate-down` | Roll back the last migration |
   256→| `make api-lint` | Run go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec) |
   257→| `make lint` | Run all linters (Go + web) |
   258→| `make audit` | Security audit on web dependencies |
   259→
   260→### Web
   261→
   262→| Command | Description |
   263→|---|---|
   264→| `make web-dev` | Start Vite dev server with HMR |
   265→| `make web-build` | TypeScript check + production build |
   266→| `make web-test` | Run Vitest unit tests |
   267→| `make web-lint` | ESLint (zero-warning policy) |
   268→| `make web-e2e` | Playwright end-to-end tests |
   269→| `make web-storybook` | Start Storybook on port 6006 |
   270→
   271→### Smoke Tests
   272→
   273→```bash
   274→# Against a running API
   275→bash scripts/smoke-test.sh
   276→
   277→# Builds the API, starts it, tests, and tears down automatically
   278→bash scripts/smoke-test-with-server.sh
   279→```
   280→
   281→---
   282→
   283→## Ports
   284→
   285→| Service | Port |
   286→|---|---|
   287→| Web dev server | 8080 |
   288→| API server | 8081 |
   289→| Dev PostgreSQL | 15432 |
   290→| Docker (all-in-one) | 8080 |
   291→| Storybook | 6006 |
   292→
   293→---
   294→
   295→## Environment Variables
   296→
   297→See `.env.example` for the full list. The essential ones:
   298→
   299→| Variable | Required | Description |
   300→|---|---|---|
   301→| `DATABASE_URL` | Yes | PostgreSQL connection string |
   302→| `JWT_SECRET` | Yes | Secret for HS256 token signing (or Supabase JWT secret) |
   303→| `JWT_PUBLIC_KEY` | No | RS256 PEM public key — overrides `JWT_SECRET` when set |
   304→| `JWT_ISSUER` | No | Expected `iss` claim (e.g. `https://<ref>.supabase.co/auth/v1`) |
   305→| `JWT_AUDIENCE` | No | Expected `aud` claim (e.g. `authenticated`) |
   306→| `GRAPHQL_PLAYGROUND` | No | Set `true` to enable GraphQL Playground at `/playground` |
   307→| `LOG_LEVEL` | No | `debug`, `info` (default), `warn`, `error` |
   308→| `LOG_FORMAT` | No | `json` (default) or `text` (dev) |
   309→| `CORS_ORIGINS` | No | Allowed origins, e.g. `http://localhost:8080` |

1→# Kapow — Claude Instructions
     2→
     3→College Power Search: a monorepo with a Go API (`api/`) and a React/TypeScript
     4→frontend (`web/`). Component-specific deep-dives are in the `@import` files below.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## Prerequisites
     9→
    10→- **Go** 1.23+
    11→- **Node.js** 22+ with **pnpm** 9+ (`npm install -g pnpm`)
    12→- **Docker** (for `make dev` — runs PostgreSQL in a container)
    13→- **PostgreSQL** 15+ with PostGIS (only if managing DB manually instead of `make dev`)
    14→- **golangci-lint** (optional): see golangci-lint docs
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Environment setup
    19→
    20→```bash
    21→cp .env.example .env   # fill in DATABASE_URL and JWT_SECRET at minimum
    22→```
    23→
    24→Required: `DATABASE_URL` — the API refuses to start without it.
    25→
    26→Ports:
    27→- **API**: `:8081`
    28→- **Web dev server**: `:8080` — proxies `/graphql` and `/api` → `localhost:8081`
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## Commands (Root Makefile)
    33→
    34→| Command | Action |
    35→|---|---|
    36→| `make dev` | Full-stack dev environment (Docker PG + air + Vite) — single command |
    37→| `make dev-clean` | Remove dev DB volume (forces fresh fixture load) |
    38→| `make dev-prune` | Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days |
    39→| `make api-build` | Compile `bin/kapow-api` |
    40→| `make api-run` | Run API server |
    41→| `make api-generate` | Regenerate gqlgen code |
    42→| `make api-test` | All Go tests (race detector) |
    43→| `make api-test-unit` | Unit tests only (`-short`) |
    44→| `make lint` | Run all linters (Go + web) |
    45→| `make audit` | Security audit on web dependencies |
    46→| `make api-lint` | go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec) |
    47→| `make api-migrate-up` | Apply pending migrations |
    48→| `make api-migrate-down` | Roll back last migration |
    49→| `make schema-gen` | Rebuild `common/.../schema.sql` |
    50→| `make web-install` | `pnpm install` |
    51→| `make web-dev` | Vite dev server (port 8080) |
    52→| `make web-build` | TypeScript check + production build |
    53→| `make web-lint` | ESLint (zero-warning mode) |
    54→| `make web-test` | Vitest unit tests |
    55→| `make web-schema-sync` | Regenerate `web/schema.graphql` + `graphql-env.d.ts` from API SDL |
    56→| `make web-clean` | Remove `dist/` and `node_modules/` |
    57→| `make smoke-test` | Search API smoke test (requires running API) |
    58→| `make test-fixture` | Validate fixture data loads against current schema (temp Docker PG) |
    59→| `make test-quick` | Fast checks: Go unit tests + TS build + ESLint (~10-20s, no DB) |
    60→| `make test-standard` | Full local: quick + web unit tests + Go lint (~30-60s, no DB) |
    61→| `make test-full` | Complete: standard + integration + e2e + smoke + fixture (requires DB + services) |
    62→| `make test-image-build` | Build all-in-one Docker test image |
    63→| `make test-image-run` | Run the Docker test image |
    64→| `make spec-gen` | Regenerate `docs/specs/generated/` reference docs |
    65→
    66→---
    67→
    68→## Testing standards
    69→
    70→### Test tiers
    71→
    72→| Tier | Command | Time | External deps | Use when |
    73→|---|---|---|---|---|
    74→| Quick | `make test-quick` | ~10-20s | None | During development |
    75→| Standard | `make test-standard` | ~30-60s | None | Before committing |
    76→| Full | `make test-full` | ~2-5min | DB + running services | Before merge |
    77→
    78→### Core rules
    79→
    80→- **Always write tests for new additions.** Tests ship with the code, not as a follow-up.
    81→- **Update existing tests when modifying behaviour.**
    82→- **Target ≥ 80% line coverage per package.** Glue/wiring packages (`cmd/server`, `router`) are exempt.
    83→- **Always run database code against a real database.** Compilation and unit tests alone are not sufficient — runtime issues like pgx type encoding mismatches only surface with a live connection.
    84→
    85→---
    86→
    87→## Issue tracking (Beads)
    88→
    89→```bash
    90→bd list                                # all open issues
    91→bd ready                               # issues with no blockers (ready to work)
    92→bd show <id>                           # detailed view with dependencies
    93→bd create --title "..." --description "..."
    94→bd update <id> --status in_progress
    95→bd close <id>
    96→```
    97→
    98→See `AGENTS.md` for issue type guidance, epic creation, dependencies, and
    99→completion workflow.
   100→
   101→---
   102→
   103→## Common task recipes
   104→
   105→### Add a config env var
   106→
   107→1. Add field to `Config` struct in `api/internal/config/config.go` with `env:"VAR_NAME"` tag
   108→2. Document it in `.env.example`
   109→3. Access via `cfg.FieldName` (config flows through `router.Deps`)
   110→
   111→### Add a database query
   112→
   113→Use pgx directly: `pool.QueryRow(ctx, sql, $1, $2, ...)`. Always pass context.
   114→Never build SQL with `fmt.Sprintf` — always use `$N` placeholders.
   115→
   116→### Add a Go package
   117→
   118→Create `api/internal/<name>/` with `package <name>`. Pass deps explicitly via
   119→structs; no `init()` globals.
   120→
   121→---
   122→
   123→## Git workflow
   124→
   125→When work is complete and verified, merge directly into `main` from the command
   126→line. Do not create pull requests unless there is an unresolvable merge problem.
   127→
   128→See `AGENTS.md` for commit conventions, worktree workflow, and session
   129→completion protocol.
   130→
   131→---
   132→
   133→## Implementation plans
   134→
   135→After plan mode approval, copy the plan from `~/.claude/plans/` into
   136→`docs/plans/` with a meaningful name (e.g., `kapow-a1b-geo-search.md`).
   137→Plans in `~/.claude/` are machine-local; the repo copy syncs across devices.
   138→See `AGENTS.md` for naming conventions and lifecycle.
   139→
   140→---
   141→
   142→## Project specs (`docs/specs/`)
   143→
   144→Comprehensive product and architecture documentation lives in `docs/specs/`.
   145→Consult these before making significant changes to understand current behaviour.
   146→
   147→**Hand-written specs** (edit when behaviour changes):
   148→
   149→| File | Covers |
   150→|---|---|
   151→| `product-overview.md` | What Kapow is, features, user stories, pages |
   152→| `graphql-api.md` | Queries, mutations, types, auth requirements |
   153→| `search-engine.md` | Field types, SQL generation, themes, data sources |
   154→| `data-model.md` | Schemas, tables, relationships, migrations |
   155→| `authentication.md` | JWT flow, Supabase, middleware, protected routes |
   156→| `frontend-architecture.md` | Pages, components, state, GraphQL client, styling |
   157→| `deployment.md` | Docker images, compose, env vars, startup sequence |
   158→
   159→**Auto-generated reference** (`docs/specs/generated/` — regenerate with `make spec-gen`):
   160→
   161→| File | Source |
   162→|---|---|
   163→| `search-fields.md` | `search.NewRegistry()` — all fields grouped by theme |
   164→| `data-sources.md` | `search.AllDataSources()` — all 12 data sources |
   165→| `graphql-schema.md` | `api/graph/schema/*.graphql` — full SDL |
   166→| `env-reference.md` | `.env.example` — all environment variables |
   167→
   168→---
   169→
   170→## What NOT to do
   171→
   172→- **Never edit** `graph/generated/generated.go` or `graph/model/models_gen.go` — run `make api-generate`
   173→- **Never commit** `.env` — only `.env.example`
   174→- **Never use `fmt.Sprintf` to build SQL** — use parameterized `$N` placeholders
   175→- **Never hardcode brand colors** — use Mantine color refs (`c="brand.9"`, `color="accent"`) or CSS vars (`var(--color-primary)` / `var(--color-accent)`)
   176→- **Never use Tailwind utility classes** — use Mantine style props or inline styles
   177→- **Never add** `node_modules/` or `dist/` to git
   178→
   179→---
   180→
   181→## README.md
   182→
   183→`README.md` is the human-facing project documentation. **Keep it up to date**
   184→when making changes that affect how someone builds, runs, or configures the
   185→project.
   186→
   187→---
   188→
   189→@api/CLAUDE.md
   190→@web/CLAUDE.md
   191→@tools/CLAUDE.md
   192→

1→# Agent Instructions
     2→
     3→This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
     4→
     5→## Quick Reference
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bd ready              # Find available work
     9→bd show <id>          # View issue details
    10→bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
    11→bd close <id>         # Complete work
    12→bd sync               # Sync with git
    13→```
    14→
    15→## Issue Title Guidelines
    16→
    17→Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
    18→is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:
    19→
    20→| Bad | Good |
    21→|---|---|
    22→| `Add support for filtering by SAT score` | `SAT score filter for search` |
    23→| `Update the search page to show result counts` | `Search result count display` |
    24→| `Fix bug where geographic radius is ignored` | `Geo radius filter ignored` |
    25→| `Implement new card component for institutions` | `Institution card component` |
    26→
    27→- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Search …", "Auth …", "GeoField …"), not filler verbs
    28→- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
    29→- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"
    30→
    31→## Creating Issues
    32→
    33→Use the correct issue type:
    34→- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
    35→- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability
    36→- `task` — scaffold, chore, or infrastructure work (e.g., project init, CI setup)
    37→- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing
    38→
    39→### Creating Epics
    40→
    41→Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:
    42→
    43→```bash
    44→bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
    45→  -d "Description of the epic" \
    46→  --waits-for-gate all-children
    47→```
    48→
    49→This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.
    50→
    51→### Creating Child Issues Under Epics
    52→
    53→Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:
    54→
    55→```bash
    56→bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l search,go \
    57→  --parent kapow-abc \
    58→  --waits-for kapow-abc \
    59→  -d "Description" \
    60→  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
    61→```
    62→
    63→The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for kapow-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.
    64→
    65→### Dependencies Between Issues
    66→
    67→Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:
    68→
    69→```bash
    70→bd dep add kapow-child kapow-dependency   # kapow-child is blocked by kapow-dependency
    71→```
    72→
    73→Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the migration must exist before the resolver can query the new column"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.
    74→
    75→## Implementation Plans
    76→
    77→Claude Code saves plans to `~/.claude/plans/` with random names (e.g.,
    78→`replicated-drifting-meerkat.md`). These are local to the machine and lost
    79→when switching devices. **After a plan is approved, copy it into the repo**
    80→so it persists across machines and sessions.
    81→
    82→### Saving a plan to the repo
    83→
    84→After exiting plan mode, copy the plan file from `~/.claude/plans/` into
    85→`docs/plans/`, renaming it to something meaningful:
    86→
    87→```bash
    88→cp ~/.claude/plans/<random-name>.md docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md
    89→```
    90→
    91→Examples:
    92→- `docs/plans/kapow-a1b-geo-search.md`
    93→- `docs/plans/auth-refactor.md` (if no issue exists yet)
    94→
    95→Use lowercase kebab-case. Prefer `<issue-id>-<short-name>` when an issue exists.
    96→
    97→### Referencing from issues
    98→
    99→Add a note to the beads issue pointing to the plan:
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→bd update <id> --notes="Plan: docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md"
   103→```
   104→
   105→### Resuming work from a plan
   106→
   107→When starting a new session, check `docs/plans/` for in-progress plans.
   108→If a beads issue references a plan file, read it before starting work.
   109→
   110→### Lifecycle
   111→
   112→- Commit and push plan files alongside the code they describe
   113→- Update plan files as the approach evolves during implementation
   114→- Delete plan files after the work is fully merged and closed
   115→
   116→## Completing an Issue
   117→
   118→An issue is not complete until:
   119→1. All code changes pass quality gates (tests, lint, build)
   120→2. Changes are committed
   121→3. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
   122→4. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`
   123→
   124→## Git Workflow
   125→
   126→When work is complete and verified (tests pass, build succeeds, lint is clean),
   127→merge directly into `main`. Only fall back to a pull request if there is an
   128→unresolvable problem merging from the command line (e.g. complex conflicts).
   129→
   130→### Commit messages and issue references
   131→
   132→When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files), list all
   133→affected issue IDs in the commit message body. This makes it easy to trace
   134→which issues were created, updated, or closed in each push.
   135→
   136→Example:
   137→```
   138→Add geo search field and update filter UI
   139→
   140→Implements proximity-based search using PostGIS ST_DWithin.
   141→
   142→Issues: kapow-a1b, kapow-c2d, kapow-e3f
   143→```
   144→
   145→### Worktree workflow
   146→
   147→When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
   148→the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
   149→worktree branch unmerged without asking.
   150→
   151→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   152→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   153→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)
   154→
   155→**After merging a worktree that touches GraphQL schema files:**
   156→- Always regenerate: `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
   157→- `.gitattributes` marks generated files with `merge=ours` — conflicts auto-resolve to the current branch, but you must regenerate to get the correct content
   158→
   159→**Worktree cleanup — do NOT do it manually:**
   160→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically when invoked with
   161→`--worktree`. **Never** run `git worktree remove` or delete the worktree
   162→directory yourself — the user is prompted to keep or remove it when the session
   163→ends. Manually removing the worktree mid-session can break the working directory
   164→and cause confusing errors.
   165→
   166→## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
   167→
   168→**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
   169→
   170→**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
   171→
   172→1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
   173→2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
   174→3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
   175→4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   176→   ```bash
   177→   git pull --rebase
   178→   bd sync
   179→   git push
   180→   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   181→   ```
   182→5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
   183→6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
   184→7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
   185→
   186→**CRITICAL RULES:**
   187→- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
   188→- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
   189→- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
   190→- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
   191→
   192→
   193→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   194→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   195→
   196→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   197→
   198→### Why bd?
   199→
   200→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   201→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
   202→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   203→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   204→
   205→### Quick Start
   206→
   207→**Check for ready work:**
   208→
   209→```bash
   210→bd ready --json
   211→```
   212→
   213→**Create new issues:**
   214→
   215→```bash
   216→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
   217→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
   218→```
   219→
   220→**Claim and update:**
   221→
   222→```bash
   223→bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
   224→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
   225→```
   226→
   227→**Complete work:**
   228→
   229→```bash
   230→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
   231→```
   232→
   233→### Issue Types
   234→
   235→- `bug` - Something broken
   236→- `feature` - New functionality
   237→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
   238→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
   239→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
   240→
   241→### Priorities
   242→
   243→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
   244→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
   245→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
   246→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
   247→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
   248→
   249→### Workflow for AI Agents
   250→
   251→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
   252→2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
   253→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
   254→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   255→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
   256→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
   257→
   258→### Auto-Sync
   259→
   260→bd automatically syncs with git:
   261→
   262→- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
   263→- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
   264→- No manual export/import needed!
   265→
   266→### Important Rules
   267→
   268→- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
   269→- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
   270→- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
   271→- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
   272→- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
   273→- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
   274→- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
   275→
   276→For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
   277→
   278→<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->
