1→# Beads Configuration File
     2→# This file configures default behavior for all bd commands in this repository
     3→# All settings can also be set via environment variables (BD_* prefix)
     4→# or overridden with command-line flags
     5→
     6→# Issue prefix for this repository (used by bd init)
     7→# If not set, bd init will auto-detect from directory name
     8→# Example: issue-prefix: "myproject" creates issues like "myproject-1", "myproject-2", etc.
     9→# issue-prefix: ""
    10→
    11→# Use no-db mode: load from JSONL, write back after each command
    12→# When true, bd will use .beads/issues.jsonl as the source of truth
    13→# instead of the Dolt database
    14→no-db: true
    15→
    16→# Enable JSON output by default
    17→# json: false
    18→
    19→# Default actor for audit trails (overridden by BD_ACTOR or --actor)
    20→# actor: ""
    21→
    22→# Debounce interval for auto-flush (can also use BEADS_FLUSH_DEBOUNCE)
    23→# flush-debounce: "5s"
    24→
    25→# Export events (audit trail) to .beads/events.jsonl on each flush/sync
    26→# When enabled, new events are appended incrementally using a high-water mark.
    27→# Use 'bd export --events' to trigger manually regardless of this setting.
    28→# events-export: false
    29→
    30→# Git branch for beads commits (bd sync will commit to this branch)
    31→# IMPORTANT: Set this for team projects so all clones use the same sync branch.
    32→# This setting persists across clones (unlike database config which is gitignored).
    33→# Can also use BEADS_SYNC_BRANCH env var for local override.
    34→# If not set, bd sync will require you to run 'bd config set sync.branch <branch>'.
    35→# sync-branch: "beads-sync"
    36→
    37→# Multi-repo configuration (experimental - bd-307)
    38→# Allows hydrating from multiple repositories and routing writes to the correct JSONL
    39→# repos:
    40→#   primary: "."  # Primary repo (where this database lives)
    41→#   additional:   # Additional repos to hydrate from (read-only)
    42→#     - ~/beads-planning  # Personal planning repo
    43→#     - ~/work-planning   # Work planning repo
    44→
    45→# Integration settings (access with 'bd config get/set')
    46→# These are stored in the database, not in this file:
    47→# - jira.url
    48→# - jira.project
    49→# - linear.url
    50→# - linear.api-key
    51→# - github.org
    52→# - github.repo

1→# Kapow — Claude Instructions
     2→
     3→College Power Search: a monorepo with a Go API (`api/`) and a React/TypeScript
     4→frontend (`web/`). Component-specific deep-dives are in the `@import` files below.
     5→
     6→---
     7→
     8→## Prerequisites
     9→
    10→- **Go** 1.23+
    11→- **Node.js** 22+ with **pnpm** 9+ (`npm install -g pnpm`)
    12→- **Docker** (for `make dev` — runs PostgreSQL in a container)
    13→- **PostgreSQL** 15+ with PostGIS (only if managing DB manually instead of `make dev`)
    14→- **golangci-lint** (optional): see golangci-lint docs
    15→
    16→---
    17→
    18→## Environment setup
    19→
    20→```bash
    21→cp .env.example .env   # fill in DATABASE_URL and JWT_SECRET at minimum
    22→```
    23→
    24→Required: `DATABASE_URL` — the API refuses to start without it.
    25→
    26→Ports:
    27→- **API**: `:8081`
    28→- **Web dev server**: `:8080` — proxies `/graphql` and `/api` → `localhost:8081`
    29→
    30→---
    31→
    32→## Commands (Root Makefile)
    33→
    34→| Command | Action |
    35→|---|---|
    36→| `make dev` | Full-stack dev environment (Docker PG + air + Vite) — single command |
    37→| `make dev-clean` | Remove dev DB volume (forces fresh fixture load) |
    38→| `make dev-prune` | Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days |
    39→| `make api-build` | Compile `bin/kapow-api` |
    40→| `make api-run` | Run API server |
    41→| `make api-generate` | Regenerate gqlgen code |
    42→| `make api-test` | All Go tests (race detector) |
    43→| `make api-test-unit` | Unit tests only (`-short`) |
    44→| `make lint` | Run all linters (Go + web) |
    45→| `make audit` | Security audit on web dependencies |
    46→| `make api-lint` | go vet + golangci-lint (includes gosec) |
    47→| `make api-migrate-up` | Apply pending migrations |
    48→| `make api-migrate-down` | Roll back last migration |
    49→| `make schema-gen` | Rebuild `common/.../schema.sql` |
    50→| `make web-install` | `pnpm install` |
    51→| `make web-dev` | Vite dev server (port 8080) |
    52→| `make web-build` | TypeScript check + production build |
    53→| `make web-lint` | ESLint (zero-warning mode) |
    54→| `make web-test` | Vitest unit tests |
    55→| `make web-schema-sync` | Regenerate `web/schema.graphql` + `graphql-env.d.ts` from API SDL |
    56→| `make web-clean` | Remove `dist/` and `node_modules/` |
    57→| `make web-test-e2e` | All Playwright E2E tests (auto-starts dev server) |
    58→| `make web-test-e2e-critical` | Only `@critical` tier E2E tests |
    59→| `make smoke-test` | Search API smoke test (requires running API) |
    60→| `make test-fixture` | Validate fixture data loads against current schema (temp Docker PG) |
    61→| `make test-quick` | Fast checks: Go unit tests + TS build + ESLint (~10-20s, no DB) |
    62→| `make test-standard` | Full local: quick + web unit tests + Go lint (~30-60s, no DB) |
    63→| `make test-full` | Complete: standard + integration + e2e + smoke + fixture (requires DB + services) |
    64→| `make test-image-build` | Build all-in-one Docker test image |
    65→| `make test-image-run` | Run the Docker test image |
    66→| `make spec-gen` | Regenerate `docs/specs/generated/` reference docs |
    67→
    68→---
    69→
    70→## Testing standards
    71→
    72→### Test tiers
    73→
    74→| Tier | Command | Time | External deps | Use when |
    75→|---|---|---|---|---|
    76→| Quick | `make test-quick` | ~10-20s | None | During development |
    77→| Standard | `make test-standard` | ~30-60s | None | Before committing |
    78→| Full | `make test-full` | ~2-5min | DB + running services | Before merge |
    79→
    80→### Core rules
    81→
    82→- **Always write tests for new additions.** Tests ship with the code, not as a follow-up.
    83→- **Update existing tests when modifying behaviour.**
    84→- **Target ≥ 80% line coverage per package.** Glue/wiring packages (`cmd/server`, `router`) are exempt.
    85→- **Always run database code against a real database.** Compilation and unit tests alone are not sufficient — runtime issues like pgx type encoding mismatches only surface with a live connection.
    86→
    87→---
    88→
    89→## Issue tracking (Beads)
    90→
    91→```bash
    92→bd list                                # all open issues
    93→bd ready                               # issues with no blockers (ready to work)
    94→bd show <id>                           # detailed view with dependencies
    95→bd create --title "..." --description "..."
    96→bd update <id> --status in_progress
    97→bd close <id>
    98→```
    99→
   100→See `AGENTS.md` for issue type guidance, epic creation, dependencies, and
   101→completion workflow.
   102→
   103→---
   104→
   105→## Common task recipes
   106→
   107→### Add a config env var
   108→
   109→1. Add field to `Config` struct in `api/internal/config/config.go` with `env:"VAR_NAME"` tag
   110→2. Document it in `.env.example`
   111→3. Access via `cfg.FieldName` (config flows through `router.Deps`)
   112→
   113→### Add a database query
   114→
   115→Use pgx directly: `pool.QueryRow(ctx, sql, $1, $2, ...)`. Always pass context.
   116→Never build SQL with `fmt.Sprintf` — always use `$N` placeholders.
   117→
   118→### Add a Go package
   119→
   120→Create `api/internal/<name>/` with `package <name>`. Pass deps explicitly via
   121→structs; no `init()` globals.
   122→
   123→---
   124→
   125→## Git workflow
   126→
   127→When work is complete and verified, merge directly into `main` from the command
   128→line. Do not create pull requests unless there is an unresolvable merge problem.
   129→
   130→See `AGENTS.md` for commit conventions, worktree workflow, and session
   131→completion protocol.
   132→
   133→---
   134→
   135→## Implementation plans
   136→
   137→After plan mode approval, copy the plan from `~/.claude/plans/` into
   138→`docs/plans/` with a meaningful name (e.g., `kapow-a1b-geo-search.md`).
   139→Plans in `~/.claude/` are machine-local; the repo copy syncs across devices.
   140→See `AGENTS.md` for naming conventions and lifecycle.
   141→
   142→---
   143→
   144→## Project specs (`docs/specs/`)
   145→
   146→Comprehensive product and architecture documentation lives in `docs/specs/`.
   147→Consult these before making significant changes to understand current behaviour.
   148→
   149→**Hand-written specs** (edit when behaviour changes):
   150→
   151→| File | Covers |
   152→|---|---|
   153→| `product-overview.md` | What Kapow is, features, user stories, pages |
   154→| `graphql-api.md` | Queries, mutations, types, auth requirements |
   155→| `search-engine.md` | Field types, SQL generation, themes, data sources |
   156→| `data-model.md` | Schemas, tables, relationships, migrations |
   157→| `authentication.md` | JWT flow, Supabase, middleware, protected routes |
   158→| `frontend-architecture.md` | Pages, components, state, GraphQL client, styling |
   159→| `deployment.md` | Docker images, compose, env vars, startup sequence |
   160→
   161→**Auto-generated reference** (`docs/specs/generated/` — regenerate with `make spec-gen`):
   162→
   163→| File | Source |
   164→|---|---|
   165→| `search-fields.md` | `search.NewRegistry()` — all fields grouped by theme |
   166→| `data-sources.md` | `search.AllDataSources()` — all 12 data sources |
   167→| `graphql-schema.md` | `api/graph/schema/*.graphql` — full SDL |
   168→| `env-reference.md` | `.env.example` — all environment variables |
   169→
   170→---
   171→
   172→## What NOT to do
   173→
   174→- **Never edit** `graph/generated/generated.go` or `graph/model/models_gen.go` — run `make api-generate`
   175→- **Never commit** `.env` — only `.env.example`
   176→- **Never use `fmt.Sprintf` to build SQL** — use parameterized `$N` placeholders
   177→- **Never hardcode brand colors** — use Mantine color refs (`c="brand.9"`, `color="accent"`) or CSS vars (`var(--color-primary)` / `var(--color-accent)`)
   178→- **Never use Tailwind utility classes** — use Mantine style props or inline styles
   179→- **Never add** `node_modules/` or `dist/` to git
   180→
   181→---
   182→
   183→## README.md
   184→
   185→`README.md` is the human-facing project documentation. **Keep it up to date**
   186→when making changes that affect how someone builds, runs, or configures the
   187→project.
   188→
   189→---
   190→
   191→@api/CLAUDE.md
   192→@web/CLAUDE.md
   193→@tools/CLAUDE.md
   194→

1→# Agent Instructions
     2→
     3→This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
     4→
     5→## Quick Reference
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bd ready              # Find available work
     9→bd show <id>          # View issue details
    10→bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
    11→bd close <id>         # Complete work
    12→bd sync               # Sync with git
    13→```
    14→
    15→## Issue Title Guidelines
    16→
    17→Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
    18→is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:
    19→
    20→| Bad | Good |
    21→|---|---|
    22→| `Add support for filtering by SAT score` | `SAT score filter for search` |
    23→| `Update the search page to show result counts` | `Search result count display` |
    24→| `Fix bug where geographic radius is ignored` | `Geo radius filter ignored` |
    25→| `Implement new card component for institutions` | `Institution card component` |
    26→
    27→- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Search …", "Auth …", "GeoField …"), not filler verbs
    28→- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
    29→- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"
    30→
    31→## Creating Issues
    32→
    33→Use the correct issue type:
    34→- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
    35→- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability
    36→- `task` — scaffold, chore, or infrastructure work (e.g., project init, CI setup)
    37→- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing
    38→
    39→### Creating Epics
    40→
    41→Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:
    42→
    43→```bash
    44→bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
    45→  -d "Description of the epic" \
    46→  --waits-for-gate all-children
    47→```
    48→
    49→This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.
    50→
    51→### Creating Child Issues Under Epics
    52→
    53→Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:
    54→
    55→```bash
    56→bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l search,go \
    57→  --parent kapow-abc \
    58→  --waits-for kapow-abc \
    59→  -d "Description" \
    60→  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
    61→```
    62→
    63→The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for kapow-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.
    64→
    65→### Dependencies Between Issues
    66→
    67→Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:
    68→
    69→```bash
    70→bd dep add kapow-child kapow-dependency   # kapow-child is blocked by kapow-dependency
    71→```
    72→
    73→Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the migration must exist before the resolver can query the new column"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.
    74→
    75→## Implementation Plans
    76→
    77→Claude Code saves plans to `~/.claude/plans/` with random names (e.g.,
    78→`replicated-drifting-meerkat.md`). These are local to the machine and lost
    79→when switching devices. **After a plan is approved, copy it into the repo**
    80→so it persists across machines and sessions.
    81→
    82→### Saving a plan to the repo
    83→
    84→After exiting plan mode, copy the plan file from `~/.claude/plans/` into
    85→`docs/plans/`, renaming it to something meaningful:
    86→
    87→```bash
    88→cp ~/.claude/plans/<random-name>.md docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md
    89→```
    90→
    91→Examples:
    92→- `docs/plans/kapow-a1b-geo-search.md`
    93→- `docs/plans/auth-refactor.md` (if no issue exists yet)
    94→
    95→Use lowercase kebab-case. Prefer `<issue-id>-<short-name>` when an issue exists.
    96→
    97→### Referencing from issues
    98→
    99→Add a note to the beads issue pointing to the plan:
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→bd update <id> --notes="Plan: docs/plans/<issue-id>-<short-name>.md"
   103→```
   104→
   105→### Resuming work from a plan
   106→
   107→When starting a new session, check `docs/plans/` for in-progress plans.
   108→If a beads issue references a plan file, read it before starting work.
   109→
   110→### Lifecycle
   111→
   112→- Commit and push plan files alongside the code they describe
   113→- Update plan files as the approach evolves during implementation
   114→- Delete plan files after the work is fully merged and closed
   115→
   116→## Completing an Issue
   117→
   118→An issue is not complete until:
   119→1. All code changes pass quality gates (tests, lint, build)
   120→2. Changes are committed
   121→3. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
   122→4. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`
   123→
   124→## Git Workflow
   125→
   126→When work is complete and verified (tests pass, build succeeds, lint is clean),
   127→merge directly into `main`. Only fall back to a pull request if there is an
   128→unresolvable problem merging from the command line (e.g. complex conflicts).
   129→
   130→### Commit messages and issue references
   131→
   132→When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files), list all
   133→affected issue IDs in the commit message body. This makes it easy to trace
   134→which issues were created, updated, or closed in each push.
   135→
   136→Example:
   137→```
   138→Add geo search field and update filter UI
   139→
   140→Implements proximity-based search using PostGIS ST_DWithin.
   141→
   142→Issues: kapow-a1b, kapow-c2d, kapow-e3f
   143→```
   144→
   145→### Worktree workflow
   146→
   147→When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
   148→the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
   149→worktree branch unmerged without asking.
   150→
   151→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   152→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   153→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)
   154→
   155→**After merging a worktree that touches GraphQL schema files:**
   156→- Always regenerate: `make api-generate && make web-schema-sync`
   157→- `.gitattributes` marks generated files with `merge=ours` — conflicts auto-resolve to the current branch, but you must regenerate to get the correct content
   158→
   159→**Worktree cleanup — do NOT do it manually:**
   160→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically when invoked with
   161→`--worktree`. **Never** run `git worktree remove` or delete the worktree
   162→directory yourself — the user is prompted to keep or remove it when the session
   163→ends. Manually removing the worktree mid-session can break the working directory
   164→and cause confusing errors.
   165→
   166→## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
   167→
   168→**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
   169→
   170→**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
   171→
   172→1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
   173→2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
   174→3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
   175→4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
   176→   ```bash
   177→   git pull --rebase
   178→   bd sync
   179→   git push
   180→   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
   181→   ```
   182→5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
   183→6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
   184→7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
   185→
   186→**CRITICAL RULES:**
   187→- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
   188→- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
   189→- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
   190→- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
   191→
   192→
   193→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   194→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   195→
   196→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   197→
   198→### Why bd?
   199→
   200→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   201→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
   202→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   203→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   204→
   205→### Quick Start
   206→
   207→**Check for ready work:**
   208→
   209→```bash
   210→bd ready --json
   211→```
   212→
   213→**Create new issues:**
   214→
   215→```bash
   216→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t bug|feature|task -p 0-4 --json
   217→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
   218→```
   219→
   220→**Claim and update:**
   221→
   222→```bash
   223→bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
   224→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
   225→```
   226→
   227→**Complete work:**
   228→
   229→```bash
   230→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
   231→```
   232→
   233→### Issue Types
   234→
   235→- `bug` - Something broken
   236→- `feature` - New functionality
   237→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
   238→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
   239→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
   240→
   241→### Priorities
   242→
   243→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
   244→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
   245→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
   246→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
   247→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
   248→
   249→### Workflow for AI Agents
   250→
   251→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
   252→2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
   253→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
   254→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   255→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
   256→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
   257→
   258→### Auto-Sync
   259→
   260→bd automatically syncs with git:
   261→
   262→- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
   263→- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
   264→- No manual export/import needed!
   265→
   266→### Important Rules
   267→
   268→- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
   269→- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
   270→- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
   271→- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
   272→- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
   273→- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
   274→- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
   275→
   276→For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
   277→
   278→<!-- END BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   279→

1→## Kapow Monorepo Makefile — delegates to component Makefiles
     2→## Usage: make <target>
     3→
     4→# Load .env if present — makes vars available to shell commands in recipes

     6→export VITE_SUPABASE_URL VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY

     8→.PHONY: api-build api-run api-generate api-test api-test-unit \
     9→        api-lint api-migrate-up api-migrate-down api-clean schema-gen \
    10→        web-install web-dev web-build web-lint web-clean web-test web-test-coverage web-e2e web-test-e2e web-test-e2e-critical web-screenshots web-storybook \
    11→        web-schema-sync \
    12→        lint audit hadolint \
    13→        smoke-test smoke-test-with-server \
    14→        api-image-build web-image-build test-image-build test-image-run \
    15→        tools-build tools-test \

    17→        compose-up compose-down compose-logs \
    18→        test-quick test-standard test-full test-fixture \

    20→        build-full \
    21→        spec-gen \

   119→	cat api/graph/schema/schema.graphql \
   120→	    api/graph/schema/auth.graphql \

   140→## hadolint: Lint all Dockerfiles with hadolint (runs via Docker)
   141→hadolint:
   142→	@find . -name 'Dockerfile*' -not -path '*/node_modules/*' -not -path '*/dist/*' | sort | \
   143→		while read -r f; do \
   144→			echo "Linting $$f"; \
   145→			docker run --rm -i hadolint/hadolint < "$$f" || exit 1; \
   146→		done

   156→## api-image-build: Build the standalone API production Docker image

   158→	docker build -f api/Dockerfile \
   159→		--build-arg VERSION=$$(git describe --tags --always --dirty 2>/dev/null || echo dev) \
   160→		--build-arg BUILD_TIME=$$(date -u +%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%SZ) \

   168→		$$([ -n "$$VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY" ] && echo "--build-arg VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY=$$VITE_SUPABASE_ANON_KEY") \

   190→## dev-clean: Remove this checkout's dev database volume (forces fresh fixture load on next `make dev`)
   191→dev-clean:
   192→	@HASH=$$(echo "$$PWD" | md5sum | cut -c1-8); \
   193→	VOL="kapow-dev-pgdata-$$HASH"; \
   194→	CONT="kapow-dev-$$HASH"; \
   195→	docker rm -f "$$CONT" 2>/dev/null || true; \
   196→	if docker volume inspect "$$VOL" >/dev/null 2>&1; then \
   197→		docker volume rm "$$VOL"; \
   198→		echo "Removed volume $$VOL"; \
   199→	else \
   200→		echo "Volume $$VOL does not exist"; \
   201→	fi

   226→## dev-prune: Remove kapow-dev volumes older than 30 days
   227→dev-prune:
   228→	@CUTOFF=$$(date -d '30 days ago' +%s 2>/dev/null || date -v-30d +%s); \
   229→	for vol in $$(docker volume ls --filter label=kapow-dev=true -q 2>/dev/null); do \
   230→		CREATED=$$(docker volume inspect "$$vol" --format '{{.CreatedAt}}' 2>/dev/null); \
   231→		VOL_TS=$$(date -d "$$CREATED" +%s 2>/dev/null || date -j -f "%Y-%m-%dT%H:%M:%S" "$$CREATED" +%s 2>/dev/null || echo 0); \
   232→		if [ "$$VOL_TS" -lt "$$CUTOFF" ]; then \
   233→			echo "Removing old volume: $$vol (created $$CREATED)"; \
   234→			docker volume rm "$$vol" 2>/dev/null || true; \
   235→		fi; \
   236→	done; \
   237→	echo "Prune complete."

   275→## help: List all available targets
   276→help:
   277→	@ESSENTIAL="api-generate build-full dev help lint test-image-run test-full test-quick test-standard web-schema-sync"; \
   278→	echo ""; \
   279→	echo "Usage: make <target>"; \
   280→	echo ""; \
   281→	echo "Essential targets:"; \
   282→	echo ""; \
   283→	grep -hE '^## [a-z]' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) | sed 's/## //' | sort -t: -k1,1 | \
   284→		while IFS=: read -r target desc; do \
   285→			for e in $$ESSENTIAL; do \
   286→				if [ "$$target" = "$$e" ]; then \
   287→					printf "  %-24s %s\n" "$$target" "$$desc"; \
   288→					break; \
   289→				fi; \
   290→			done; \
   291→		done; \
   292→	echo ""; \
   293→	echo "All targets:"; \
   294→	echo ""; \
   295→	grep -hE '^## [a-z]' $(MAKEFILE_LIST) | sed 's/## //' | sort -t: -k1,1 | \
   296→		while IFS=: read -r target desc; do \
   297→			SKIP=false; \
   298→			for e in $$ESSENTIAL; do \
   299→				if [ "$$target" = "$$e" ]; then SKIP=true; break; fi; \
   300→			done; \
   301→			if [ "$$SKIP" = "false" ]; then \
   302→				printf "  %-24s %s\n" "$$target" "$$desc"; \
   303→			fi; \
   304→		done

     2→# This file configures default behavior for all bd commands in this repository
     3→# All settings can also be set via environment variables (BD_* prefix)
     4→# or overridden with command-line flags
     5→
     6→# Issue prefix for this repository (used by bd init)
     7→# If not set, bd init will auto-detect from directory name
     8→# Example: issue-prefix: "myproject" creates issues like "myproject-1", "myproject-2", etc.
     9→issue-prefix: str
    10→
    11→# Dolt is the database backend. Auto-start ensures bd commands work
    12→# without manually running 'bd dolt start' first.
    13→dolt:
    14→  auto-start: true
    15→
    16→# Enable JSON output by default
    17→# json: false
    18→
    19→# Default actor for audit trails (overridden by BD_ACTOR or --actor)
    20→# actor: ""
    21→
    22→# Export events (audit trail) to .beads/events.jsonl on each flush/sync
    23→# When enabled, new events are appended incrementally using a high-water mark.
    24→# Use 'bd export --events' to trigger manually regardless of this setting.
    25→# events-export: false
    26→
    27→# Multi-repo configuration (experimental - bd-307)
    28→# Allows hydrating from multiple repositories and routing writes to the correct JSONL
    29→# repos:
    30→#   primary: "."  # Primary repo (where this database lives)
    31→#   additional:   # Additional repos to hydrate from (read-only)
    32→#     - ~/beads-planning  # Personal planning repo

    35→# Integration settings (access with 'bd config get/set')

    42→# - github.repo

1→# Shatter v2
     2→
     3→Automatic exploratory testing via concolic execution. Rust core engine with language-specific frontends (TypeScript, Go, Rust) communicating via JSON-over-stdio protocol.
     4→
     5→See `PLAN.md` for the full architecture and implementation roadmap.
     6→
     7→## Prerequisites
     8→
     9→For local dev: Rust toolchain, Node.js 22+, Go 1.24+, libclang. Run `./scripts/configure-bindgen.sh` if Z3 build fails. Devcontainer includes everything.
    10→
    11→## Code Quality Standards
    12→
    13→- All Rust code must pass `cargo clippy` with no warnings
    14→- All TypeScript code must pass strict mode (`strict: true` in tsconfig)
    15→- All Go code must pass `go vet` and `golangci-lint`
    16→- No `unwrap()` in Rust library code — use `Result` and `?`
    17→- No `any` type in TypeScript — use proper typing
    18→- Dependencies flow in one direction: cli → core, frontends → protocol
    19→- Integration tests use known-answer functions with expected branches and triggering inputs
    20→- Frontend protocol handlers have round-trip tests (serialize → deserialize → verify)
    21→- Regression snapshots are checked into the repo and verified in CI
    22→- **Bug fixes require a reproduction test first** — write an automated test that demonstrates the bug (must fail), then fix the code and verify the test passes. Never attempt a fix without a failing test.
    23→- **No magic numbers or string literals** — define named constants for default values, timeouts, error codes, capability lists, and any value that appears in both production code and tests. Tests must reference the constant, not duplicate the literal. Each language has a canonical location for constants (see per-language conventions below).
    24→- **No hardcoded absolute paths** — never write a literal absolute path (e.g. `"/home/user/project/..."`) in code, tests, output artifacts, or documentation. Runtime-computed absolute paths are fine and often necessary — use `path.resolve(...)` (TS), `filepath.Abs` (Go), or `canonicalize()` / `env::current_dir` (Rust). The rule prohibits hardcoded paths that bake in machine-specific locations, not absolute paths in general.
    25→- **Parallel code paths must maintain parity** — when two functions process the same domain (e.g. `buildSymExpr` and `buildSymExprWithFlow`, random explorer and concolic orchestrator, CLI wiring for `--concolic` vs default), they must handle the same cases. When adding a capability to one path, check the other. When adding a new AST node type, CLI flag, or config field, grep for the parallel code path and update it too.
    26→- **Rust contracts (`#[requires]`, `#[ensures]`)** — use only where ALL THREE hold: (1) trust boundary (Z3 FFI, subprocess JSON, cross-collection indices), (2) type gap (can't encode as a type), (3) silent corruption (violation propagates as wrong results, not panics). Additionally, there must be a plausible bug the contract catches that proptest wouldn't. See `shatter-core/CLAUDE.md` for the full policy and qualifying sites.
    27→
    28→See `/rust-conventions`, `/ts-conventions`, `/go-conventions` skills for detailed per-language standards.
    29→
    30→### File Format Standards
    31→
    32→- **YAML** is the format of choice for structured data and configuration files (not TOML, JSON, or XML)
    33→- **Markdown** is the format of choice for formatted text (documentation, plans, notes, specs)
    34→
    35→### Inline Documentation
    36→
    37→Comments explain **why** or document **non-obvious contracts** — never restate what the code already says. If a name needs a comment, choose a better name first.
    38→
    39→**What to document:**
    40→- Public API contracts: preconditions, postconditions, error behavior, ownership semantics
    41→- Non-obvious design choices: why an algorithm was chosen, why a field exists, why ordering matters
    42→- Known-answer test fixtures: expected branches, triggering inputs, and edge cases (see `examples/go/04-nested-control-flow.go` for the model)
    43→- `#[allow(...)]` / `@ts-ignore`: always explain why the suppression is needed
    44→
    45→**What NOT to document:**
    46→- What the code does when the code already says it (`// returns the sum` above `fn sum()`)
    47→- Type information visible in the signature (`// takes a string` above `fn foo(s: string)`)
    48→- Existence of language constructs (`// uses a switch statement`, `// is a simple struct`)
    49→
    50→**The delete test:** If you can delete a comment and the code is equally clear, delete it.
    51→
    52→**Bad → Good examples (from this codebase):**
    53→
    54→```go
    55→// BAD: restates syntax
    56→// SwitchOnString uses a switch statement.
    57→func SwitchOnString(color string) int {
    58→
    59→// GOOD: documents the test contract
    60→// SwitchOnString — 4 branches: "red"→1, "green"→2, "blue"→3, default→0.
    61→// Analyzer should detect all four arms and the string-equality conditions.
    62→func SwitchOnString(color string) int {
    63→```
    64→
    65→```go
    66→// BAD: restates the type definition
    67→// Point is a simple struct.
    68→type Point struct {
    69→
    70→// GOOD: no comment (the struct is self-describing). Or if it's testdata:
    71→// Point — test fixture for struct-field access analysis.
    72→type Point struct {
    73→```
    74→
    75→```rust
    76→// BAD: restates the signature
    77→/// Returns boundary values for the given type.
    78→pub fn boundaries_for(ty: &ParamType) -> Vec<BoundaryValue> {
    79→
    80→// GOOD: documents the contract
    81→/// Returns boundary values applicable to `ty`, ordered by category
    82→/// (limits first, then zeroes, then special values like NaN/empty).
    83→pub fn boundaries_for(ty: &ParamType) -> Vec<BoundaryValue> {
    84→```
    85→
    86→### Test Tiers
    87→
    88→| Tier | Command | Use when |
    89→|---|---|---|
    90→| Quick | `cargo test` | During development |
    91→| Standard | `cargo test && cargo clippy -- -D warnings` | Before committing |
    92→| Full | Standard + `cd shatter-ts && npm test` + `cd shatter-go && go test ./...` + `cd shatter-rust && cargo test` | Before merge or when touching protocol definitions |
    93→| E2E | Full + `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` | After changing solver, instrumentor, explorer, orchestrator, or string ops |
    94→| Walkthrough | `bash demo/walkthrough.sh --auto --delay 0` | After changing CLI output, protocol, frontend execution, or example files |
    95→
    96→**E2E gate**: The E2E concolic tests (`shatter-core/tests/e2e_concolic.rs`) run the real TS frontend subprocess through analyze → instrument → explore → Z3 solve. They are the **only tests that validate the full pipeline end-to-end**. Unit tests alone are insufficient — a module can pass all its own tests while being silently disconnected from the pipeline (see "Completion checklist" below). Run E2E tests after any change to:
    97→- Solver logic (`solver.rs`, `string-ops.yaml`, `build.rs`)
    98→- Instrumentor (`instrumentor.ts`, especially `buildSymExpr*` functions)
    99→- Explorer or orchestrator (`explorer.rs`, `orchestrator.rs`)
   100→- Protocol types that affect execute responses (`protocol.rs`, `protocol.ts`)
   101→- CLI wiring that passes config to explorers (`main.rs`)
   102→
   103→**Walkthrough gate**: The walkthrough exercises the full pipeline end-to-end (analyze, explore, scan, export, spec). Run it after any change to CLI commands, frontend handlers, protocol types, or example files. The walkthrough prints an **ERROR SUMMARY** at the end and exits with code 1 if any step produced errors — check this summary, investigate failures, and fix before merging. Known exceptions: `stale` exit code 1 is informational (means "some functions are stale"), and scan errors for `11-opaque-types.ts` and `12-external-deps.ts` are expected.
   104→
   105→### Formal Methods & Verification
   106→
   107→Four complementary tools, each with a distinct role. PBT is the workhorse; the others fill gaps PBT can't reach.
   108→
   109→| Tool | Role | When to use |
   110→|---|---|---|
   111→| **Property-based testing** | Invariant discovery, regression prevention | Any non-trivial public function with invariants |
   112→| **Native fuzzing** | Crash resistance at parsing boundaries | Code that deserializes untrusted input |
   113→| **Contracts** (`contracts` crate) | Runtime assertions at trust boundaries | Only where Rust's type system can't express the invariant (see below) |
   114→| **Kani model checking** (deferred — P4) | Exhaustive verification of critical algorithms | Highest-stakes properties only (solver correctness). Not yet in use. |
   115→
   116→#### Property-Based Testing (primary strategy)
   117→
   118→Every component uses PBT: **proptest** (Rust), **fast-check** (TypeScript), **rapid** (Go). PBT is not optional decoration — it is a primary testing strategy alongside unit tests and E2E tests.
   119→
   120→**When adding or modifying a public function**, add property tests that cover its core invariants:
   121→- **Roundtrip properties**: serialize → deserialize → equality (table stakes — always include for serializable types)
   122→- **Semantic invariants**: "output types match input types", "length is preserved", "ordering is maintained" — these catch real bugs that fixed examples miss
   123→- **Pipeline composition**: test functions composed together, not just in isolation. The solver bridge (constraints → solve → overlay) and the explore loop (execute → classify → worklist) are especially important.
   124→- **Negative properties**: malformed/adversarial input never causes panics
   125→
   126→**Shared generators**: reuse `test_arbitraries.rs` (Rust), `arbSymExpr`/`arbTypeInfo` (TS), `genTypeInfo` (Go). Don't reinvent type generators per test file.
   127→
   128→**Coverage target**: every module that handles untrusted input, crosses an FFI boundary, or maintains state should have PBT coverage of its core invariants — not just serialization.
   129→
   130→See sub-crate CLAUDE.md files for per-component PBT priorities (`shatter-core`, `shatter-ts`, `shatter-go`).
   131→
   132→#### Native Fuzzing
   133→
   134→- **Go**: `testing.F` in `*_fuzz_test.go` files — byte-level mutation for crash/panic discovery at parsing boundaries. Seed corpus from existing test fixtures.
   135→- **Rust**: `cargo-fuzz` for deserialization boundaries.
   136→- Add a fuzz target for any code that deserializes untrusted input (protocol messages, subprocess JSON).
   137→
   138→#### Contracts
   139→
   140→High bar. Use `#[requires]`/`#[ensures]` only where ALL THREE hold: (1) trust boundary, (2) type gap, (3) silent corruption. See `shatter-core/CLAUDE.md` for the full policy, qualifying sites, and what does NOT qualify.
   141→
   142→#### Anti-Patterns
   143→
   144→- Contracts that restate type signatures — use the type system instead
   145→- Proptest for trivial getters/setters — specific examples are clearer
   146→- PBT that only tests serialization roundtrips without semantic invariants — roundtrips are table stakes, not the goal
   147→- Duplicating generators across test files — use shared generators in `test_arbitraries.rs` / `arbSymExpr` / `genTypeInfo`
   148→
   149→### Completion Checklist
   150→
   151→Before declaring any feature or bug fix **done**, verify:
   152→
   153→1. **Unit tests pass** — the module's own tests (Quick tier)
   154→2. **Clippy clean** — no warnings in the changed crate (Standard tier)
   155→3. **Property tests adequate** — if adding/modifying a public function, include proptest/fast-check/rapid properties covering its core invariants (not just serialization roundtrips)
   156→4. **Cross-language tests pass** — if touching protocol types (Full tier)
   157→5. **E2E pipeline works** — if touching any component in the analyze → instrument → execute → solve chain, run `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` and verify the pipeline still discovers expected branches
   158→6. **Walkthrough passes** — if touching CLI output or example files
   159→
   160→**Why this matters:** This project has multiple code paths that process the same data (random explorer vs. concolic orchestrator, `buildSymExpr` vs. `buildSymExprWithFlow`, CLI wiring for different explorer modes). Features that work on one path are routinely broken on others. The E2E tests are the only reliable way to catch cross-path regressions.
   161→
   162→**A feature is not done until it works end-to-end.** Closing an issue based on unit tests alone has repeatedly led to silent pipeline breakages that compound over time. If the E2E tests don't cover your change, add a new E2E test case before closing.
   163→
   164→## What NOT to Do
   165→
   166→- **Never edit generated protocol bindings** manually — regenerate from the schema
   167→- **Never commit** `.env` or files containing secrets — only `.env.example`
   168→- **Never add** `node_modules/`, `dist/`, or `target/` to git
   169→- **Never bypass clippy warnings** with `#[allow(...)]` without a comment explaining why
   170→- **Never add a CLI command** without updating `demo/walkthrough.sh`
   171→- **Never close a pipeline feature based on unit tests alone** — run `cargo test --test e2e_concolic` to verify end-to-end behavior. Unit tests prove a module works in isolation; E2E tests prove it works in the pipeline. Both are required.
   172→- **Never hardcode absolute paths** — no literal paths like `"/home/user/..."` or `"/tmp/specific-dir/..."` in source, tests, or config. Runtime-computed absolute paths (from `canonicalize()`, `current_dir()`, `path.resolve()`, etc.) are fine.
   173→- **Never add a capability to one explorer path without checking the other** — the random explorer (`explorer.rs`) and concolic orchestrator (`orchestrator.rs`) are wired differently in `main.rs`. A feature added to one is routinely missing from the other (see str-emw6). Grep for the parallel path before declaring done.
   174→
   175→## Common Task Recipes
   176→
   177→### Add a new protocol message type
   178→
   179→1. Define the message in `shatter-core/src/protocol.rs` (Rust types + serde)
   180→2. Add round-trip serialization tests in the same module
   181→3. Implement the handler in each frontend:
   182→   - TypeScript: `shatter-ts/src/protocol.ts`
   183→   - Go: `shatter-go/protocol/`
   184→   - Rust: `shatter-rust/src/protocol.rs`
   185→4. Add round-trip tests in each frontend (serialize → deserialize → verify)
   186→
   187→### Add a new CLI command
   188→
   189→1. Add the clap subcommand in `shatter-cli/src/`
   190→2. Implement the handler, delegating to `shatter-core` for logic
   191→3. Add integration tests exercising the command
   192→4. Update `demo/walkthrough.sh` to exercise the new command
   193→
   194→### Add an integration test with known-answer functions
   195→
   196→1. Write the target function in `examples/typescript/src/` (or the relevant language)
   197→2. Document the expected branches and triggering inputs in a comment
   198→3. Write the test in `shatter-core` that invokes the engine and asserts all branches are found
   199→4. Check in a regression snapshot of the output
   200→
   201→### Frontend timeout contract
   202→
   203→All frontends MUST read the `SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT` env var (seconds) and apply it to function execution. The CLI sets this var from `--timeout` before spawning frontends.
   204→
   205→| Frontend | Default | Env var | Implementation |
   206→|---|---|---|---|
   207→| Go | 5s | `SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT` | `execTimeout()` in `instrument/executor.go` |
   208→| TypeScript | 15s | `SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT` | `getExecTimeoutMs()` in `src/executor.ts` |
   209→| Rust | 5s | `SHATTER_EXEC_TIMEOUT` | `exec_timeout_from_env()` in `src/handler.rs` (stored, not yet applied — execute is unimplemented) |
   210→
   211→Invalid values (non-numeric, zero, negative) fall back to the default silently.
   212→
   213→## Output Review
   214→
   215→After any change affecting CLI output, frontend logging, or protocol formatting, run `/walkthrough-review` to validate the output is human-readable.
   216→
   217→Update README.md when build/run/config procedures change.
   218→
   219→## Agent Workflow
   220→
   221→See `AGENTS.md` for issue tracking (beads), git workflow, and agent operational instructions.
   222→
   223→### Sprint Workflow
   224→
   225→When asked to work on ready issues in parallel, **invoke `/swarm`** (the global skill handles team/worktree/merge mechanics). For epic-based work or Shatter-specific quality gates, also invoke `/swarm-project` which adds wave scheduling via `bd swarm` and runs `/check-all` + `/walkthrough-review`.
   226→
   227→### Research Memory
   228→
   229→After researching codebase architecture or feature implementation status, save factual findings to project memory proactively — don't wait for the user to ask. Tag entries with date so stale facts can be identified later. This applies to any confirmed facts learned by reading code: what's implemented vs stubbed, how mechanisms work, which frontends support which features, etc.
   230→
   231→### Plans
   232→
   233→When a planning session produces a plan worth preserving, copy it from `~/.claude/plans/` into `docs/plans/` with a filename including the issue key and a descriptive name (e.g., `str-kapl-resilience-timeouts-memory.md`). Reference the plan from the relevant beads issue(s) via `--notes`.
   234→
   235→### Efficiency Rules
   236→
   237→- **Batch `bd show` calls**: `bd show X && echo --- && bd show Y && echo --- && bd show Z` — never sequential individual calls.
   238→- **Before `git merge`**: Always run `git branch --show-current` to verify you are on main. If not, `git checkout main` first.
   239→- **After context compaction**: Trust the summary. Do not re-run git status, git diff, git log, or test suites that the pre-compaction portion already completed.
   240→- **AskUserQuestion**: Only for decisions where the wrong choice requires significant rework. For preference questions, pick a sensible default and proceed.

1→# Agent Instructions
     2→
     3→This project uses **bd** (beads) for issue tracking. Run `bd onboard` to get started.
     4→
     5→## Quick Reference
     6→
     7→```bash
     8→bd ready              # Find available work
     9→bd show <id>          # View issue details
    10→bd update <id> --status in_progress  # Claim work
    11→bd close <id>         # Complete work
    12→bd sync               # Sync with git
    13→```
    14→
    15→## Issue Title Guidelines
    16→
    17→Titles appear in `bd list`, `bd ready`, and terminal window titles where space
    18→is limited. The **first ~20 characters** must be meaningfully descriptive:
    19→
    20→| Bad | Good |
    21→|---|---|
    22→| `Add support for symbolic integers` | `Symbolic int support` |
    23→| `Implement the protocol message for branch data` | `Branch data protocol msg` |
    24→| `Fix bug where path constraints are dropped` | `Path constraints dropped` |
    25→| `Create new frontend handler for Go` | `Go frontend handler` |
    26→
    27→- **Lead with the noun or area** ("Protocol …", "Solver …", "CLI …"), not filler verbs
    28→- **Under 50 characters** — put detail in `--description`
    29→- Avoid prefixes like "Add support for", "Update the", "Implement new"
    30→
    31→## Landing the Plane (Session Completion)
    32→
    33→**When ending a work session**, you MUST complete ALL steps below. Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds.
    34→
    35→**MANDATORY WORKFLOW:**
    36→
    37→1. **File issues for remaining work** - Create issues for anything that needs follow-up
    38→2. **Run quality gates** (if code changed) - Tests, linters, builds
    39→3. **Update issue status** - Close finished work, update in-progress items
    40→4. **PUSH TO REMOTE** - This is MANDATORY:
    41→   ```bash
    42→   git pull --rebase
    43→   bd sync
    44→   git push
    45→   git status  # MUST show "up to date with origin"
    46→   ```
    47→5. **Clean up** - Clear stashes, prune remote branches
    48→6. **Verify** - All changes committed AND pushed
    49→7. **Hand off** - Provide context for next session
    50→
    51→**CRITICAL RULES:**
    52→- Work is NOT complete until `git push` succeeds
    53→- NEVER stop before pushing - that leaves work stranded locally
    54→- NEVER say "ready to push when you are" - YOU must push
    55→- If push fails, resolve and retry until it succeeds
    56→
    57→## Creating Issues
    58→
    59→### Issue Sizing: Keep Issues Small
    60→
    61→Every issue should represent **one focused, independently completable unit of work**.
    62→A well-sized issue can be implemented, tested, and merged in a single session.
    63→
    64→**Target size:** 1–3 files changed, under ~200 lines of diff. If an issue looks
    65→bigger, break it down.
    66→
    67→**How to tell an issue is too big:**
    68→- It touches more than one module or subsystem
    69→- The description has multiple distinct deliverables or bullet points
    70→- It requires changes in both core and a frontend
    71→- You find yourself thinking "first I need to X, then Y, then Z" — each of those is its own issue
    72→- The acceptance criteria have more than 3 items
    73→
    74→**What to do instead:**
    75→- Create an **epic** for the larger goal, then create small child issues under it
    76→- Each child issue should be one logical step: "add the type", "implement the handler", "write the tests", "update the CLI"
    77→- Use `--parent` and `--waits-for` to link children to the epic
    78→- Use `bd dep add` for ordering constraints between children
    79→
    80→**Examples:**
    81→
    82→| Too big | Break into |
    83→|---|---|
    84→| `Add symbolic integer support` | Epic + `Int symbolic type`, `Int constraint gen`, `Int solver integration`, `Int round-trip tests` |
    85→| `Protocol message for branch data` | `Branch data Rust types`, `Branch data TS handler`, `Branch data Go handler` |
    86→| `Fix and test path constraint bug` | `Repro test for path constraints`, `Fix path constraint drop` |
    87→
    88→**Rule of thumb:** If you can't describe the issue in one sentence without "and",
    89→it's two issues.
    90→
    91→**Always specify `--type` explicitly** — never rely on the default. Use the correct issue type:
    92→- `feature` — delivers new user-visible or agent-visible capability (most issues are features)
    93→- `bug` — something broken that needs fixing
    94→- `epic` — a grouping container for related features/tasks. Not directly workable.
    95→- `task` — narrow infrastructure chore with no user-visible effect (CI config, project init, doc-only changes). If it adds capability, it's a `feature`.
    96→
    97→### Creating Epics
    98→
    99→Epics must use `--waits-for-gate` so they are blocked until their children complete, and do not appear in `bd ready`:
   100→
   101→```bash
   102→bd create "Epic: Feature Area" -t epic -p 2 -l label1,label2 \
   103→  -d "Description of the epic" \
   104→  --waits-for-gate all-children
   105→```
   106→
   107→This makes the epic resolve automatically when all children are closed. Never leave an epic as a bare open issue with no gate — it will pollute `bd ready`.
   108→
   109→### Creating Child Issues Under Epics
   110→
   111→Use `--parent` to place issues under an epic, and `--waits-for` to make the epic wait for the child:
   112→
   113→```bash
   114→bd create "Implement feature X" -t feature -p 1 -l core,rust \
   115→  --parent str-abc \
   116→  --waits-for str-abc \
   117→  -d "Description" \
   118→  --acceptance "Acceptance criteria"
   119→```
   120→
   121→The `--parent` flag establishes the hierarchy. The `--waits-for str-abc` flag tells the epic to wait for this child before it can close.
   122→
   123→### Dependencies Between Issues
   124→
   125→Use `bd dep add` to express ordering constraints between issues:
   126→
   127→```bash
   128→bd dep add str-child str-dependency   # str-child is blocked by str-dependency
   129→```
   130→
   131→Only add dependencies where there is a real technical ordering constraint (e.g., "the executor cannot be built before the instrumentor exists"). Do not add dependencies for soft preferences or nice-to-have ordering.
   132→
   133→### Bug Fix Policy: Reproduce Before Fixing
   134→
   135→All bugs must be reproduced in a good automated test **before** attempting any fix.
   136→The workflow is:
   137→
   138→1. Write a test that demonstrates the bug (the test must **fail**)
   139→2. Verify the test fails for the right reason
   140→3. Implement the fix
   141→4. Verify the test now **passes**
   142→
   143→This ensures every bug fix is validated by a regression test and prevents
   144→"fixes" that don't actually address the root cause.
   145→
   146→### Completing an Issue
   147→
   148→An issue is not complete until:
   149→1. All code changes pass quality gates (tests, clippy/lint, build)
   150→2. Changes are committed on a **dedicated branch for this issue only** (no other issues' changes)
   151→3. The branch is merged to `main` (one branch at a time — do not merge other branches simultaneously)
   152→4. The branch is deleted after merge
   153→5. The issue is closed with `bd close <id>`
   154→6. Changes are pushed to remote with `bd sync && git push`
   155→
   156→Do not leave stale branches. Merge and delete promptly.
   157→
   158→**Never** combine work for multiple issues on one branch. If you discover
   159→additional work while on an issue branch, create a new issue and report it to
   160→the team lead (or note it for later) — do NOT implement it yourself. Address
   161→it on a separate branch after merging the current one. Scope creep across
   162→agents is the primary cause of duplicate commits and orphan branches.
   163→
   164→## Git Workflow
   165→
   166→- Work on feature branches, not `main` directly
   167→- **One branch per issue** — never combine multiple issues in a single branch
   168→- Branch names should reference the issue: `str-<hash>-short-description`
   169→- Commits should be clean and atomic — one logical change per commit
   170→- Rebase feature branches onto `main` before merging
   171→- **Merge one branch at a time** — merge branch A to `main`, push, then merge branch B. Never batch-merge multiple branches in a single operation.
   172→- Merge to `main` directly — do NOT create pull requests
   173→- After merge, delete the feature branch both locally and remotely
   174→- Work is complete when changes are on `main` and pushed, not when a branch is pushed
   175→- **Never cherry-pick commits.** Cherry-picking creates duplicate commits with
   176→  different SHAs, making history confusing and leaving orphan branches that
   177→  appear unmerged. Always merge or rebase entire branches instead.
   178→
   179→### Commit Early, Commit Often
   180→
   181→Sessions can be interrupted at any time — context window compaction, usage limits,
   182→crashes, or network failures. **Uncommitted work is lost work.**
   183→
   184→- **Commit after every meaningful change.** Don't accumulate multiple logical
   185→  changes before committing. If you've made something work — tests pass, a
   186→  function is implemented, a bug is fixed — commit it immediately.
   187→- **Push your branch frequently.** A local commit is better than uncommitted
   188→  changes, but a pushed commit survives everything. Push after every 1–2 commits.
   189→- **Never hold work "until it's ready."** A series of small, incremental commits
   190→  on a feature branch is vastly better than one big commit that might never land.
   191→- **Teammates in worktrees**: push your branch before reporting task completion.
   192→  If the session is interrupted before the lead merges your branch, pushed work
   193→  can be recovered. Unpushed work in a worktree cannot.
   194→
   195→### Commit messages and issue references
   196→
   197→When working on a beads issue, **prefix the commit message with the issue key**.
   198→This links commits to the issue they address and makes history easy to trace.
   199→
   200→Format: `<issue-key>: <description>`
   201→
   202→Example for feature commits:
   203→```
   204→str-a1b: Add symbolic integer support to concolic engine
   205→
   206→Implements concrete+symbolic tracking for i32/i64 values with
   207→path constraint generation for branch conditions.
   208→```
   209→
   210→When a commit addresses multiple issues, use the primary issue as the prefix
   211→and list others in the body:
   212→```
   213→str-a1b: Add symbolic integer support to concolic engine
   214→
   215→Implements concrete+symbolic tracking for i32/i64 values with
   216→path constraint generation for branch conditions.
   217→
   218→Also: str-c2d, str-e3f
   219→```
   220→
   221→When a commit includes changes to beads data (`.beads/` files) without a
   222→specific feature issue, list all affected issue IDs in the body:
   223→```
   224→bd sync: close str-a1b, str-c2d
   225→
   226→Issues: str-a1b, str-c2d
   227→```
   228→
   229→### Parallel work with agent teams (preferred)
   230→
   231→For parallel work on multiple issues, use Claude Code's **agent teams** instead
   232→of manually running separate Claude instances in different worktrees. Teams
   233→coordinate through the Task tool and each teammate runs in an isolated worktree
   234→automatically (via `isolation: "worktree"` on the Task tool).
   235→
   236→**Why teams over manual worktrees:**
   237→- Claude manages concurrency — no Dolt database conflicts
   238→- Teammates coordinate via task lists and messages
   239→- Worktree lifecycle is fully automatic
   240→- The team lead merges results back to `main`
   241→
   242→**Supervision:** Always spawn teammates with `mode: "plan"`. Each teammate
   243→must write a plan and get approval from the team lead before implementing.
   244→This ensures the user can review proposed approaches before code is written.
   245→
   246→**Quick start:** Use `/swarm` to automate the full parallel workflow — triage,
   247→team setup, plan review, merge, quality gates, and close protocol.
   248→
   249→**Manual workflow (if not using `/swarm`):**
   250→```
   251→1. Create tasks for each issue (TaskCreate)
   252→2. Spawn teammates with isolation: "worktree", mode: "plan" (Task tool)
   253→3. Each teammate works on exactly ONE issue on its own branch
   254→4. Each teammate researches and proposes a plan
   255→5. Team lead reviews and approves/rejects each plan
   256→6. Approved teammates implement, team lead merges results ONE AT A TIME
   257→   (merge branch A → main → push, then merge branch B → main → push, etc.)
   258→```
   259→
   260→**Preventing duplicate work across agents:**
   261→- The team lead must assign each issue to exactly one teammate. Never assign
   262→  the same issue to multiple agents.
   263→- Teammates must check `bd show <id>` before starting — if the issue is already
   264→  `in_progress` or `closed`, stop and ask the lead for a different assignment.
   265→- The team lead must mark issues `in_progress` (with assignee) before spawning
   266→  the teammate, not after.
   267→
   268→**Post-swarm cleanup:**
   269→After all teammates finish and their branches are merged, the team lead must:
   270→1. Delete all merged worktree branches: `git branch -d <branch>`
   271→2. Verify no orphan branches remain: `git branch --no-merged main`
   272→3. Clean up worktree directories if any remain under `.claude/worktrees/`
   273→
   274→Orphan branches from interrupted swarms create confusion — they appear unmerged
   275→but contain duplicate work that already landed on `main` via a different branch.
   276→
   277→### Mandatory Pre-Completion Check
   278→
   279→Agents MUST run **`/pre-completion`** before announcing work is done — whether
   280→reporting to a team lead or to the user. This skill verifies quality gates, E2E
   281→tests, commits pushed, scope, and acceptance criteria. Do not declare completion
   282→until `/pre-completion` reports PASS.
   283→
   284→The `/pre-completion` output table is proof of completion. Agents MUST include
   285→it verbatim in their completion message. Team leads MUST reject completion
   286→announcements that lack the table or show any FAIL status.
   287→
   288→This is a hard requirement, not a suggestion. Agents that skip `/pre-completion`
   289→risk announcing "done" with failing tests, unpushed commits, or scope creep —
   290→all of which have caused regressions in this project.
   291→
   292→### Worktree isolation for teammates
   293→
   294→When spawned as a teammate with `isolation: "worktree"`, you run in a separate
   295→copy of the repo. **Violating isolation corrupts other agents' work.**
   296→
   297→**Rules:**
   298→- **Never `cd` to the main repo**. Stay in your worktree directory (under
   299→  `.claude/worktrees/`). If unsure, run `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`.
   300→- **Never `git checkout`** to switch branches. You are already on your branch.
   301→- **Never run git commands with `-C` pointing to the main repo**. All git
   302→  operations must target your worktree.
   303→- **Verify isolation** before critical operations: `git rev-parse --show-toplevel`
   304→  must return a path containing `.claude/worktrees/`.
   305→- **Commit and push only your branch**. The team lead merges to main.
   306→
   307→**Why this matters:** All worktrees share the same `.git` directory. Running
   308→`git checkout` in the main repo changes HEAD for the main working directory,
   309→which other agents and the team lead depend on. This causes branch switching
   310→races, lost commits, and merge failures.
   311→
   312→### Worktree workflow (single-agent)
   313→
   314→When working in a git worktree (e.g. started with `--worktree`), **always merge
   315→the branch back into `main` before ending the session.** Do not leave the
   316→worktree branch unmerged without asking.
   317→
   318→**Before merging a worktree branch:**
   319→- Preview conflicts first: `git merge --no-commit --no-ff <branch>` then `git merge --abort`
   320→- Rebase long-lived worktrees onto main before merging: `git rebase main` (from the worktree)
   321→
   322→**Worktree cleanup — do NOT do it manually:**
   323→Claude Code manages worktree lifecycle automatically when invoked with
   324→`--worktree`. **Never** run `git worktree remove` or delete the worktree
   325→directory yourself — the user is prompted to keep or remove it when the session
   326→ends. Manually removing the worktree mid-session can break the working directory
   327→and cause confusing errors.
   328→
   329→<!-- BEGIN BEADS INTEGRATION -->
   330→## Issue Tracking with bd (beads)
   331→
   332→**IMPORTANT**: This project uses **bd (beads)** for ALL issue tracking. Do NOT use markdown TODOs, task lists, or other tracking methods.
   333→
   334→### Why bd?
   335→
   336→- Dependency-aware: Track blockers and relationships between issues
   337→- Git-friendly: Auto-syncs to JSONL for version control
   338→- Agent-optimized: JSON output, ready work detection, discovered-from links
   339→- Prevents duplicate tracking systems and confusion
   340→
   341→### Quick Start
   342→
   343→**Check for ready work:**
   344→
   345→```bash
   346→bd ready --json
   347→```
   348→
   349→**Create new issues:**
   350→
   351→```bash
   352→bd create "Issue title" --description="Detailed context" -t feature -p 0-4 --json  # ALWAYS specify -t
   353→bd create "Issue title" --description="What this issue is about" -t bug -p 1 --deps discovered-from:bd-123 --json
   354→```
   355→
   356→**Claim and update:**
   357→
   358→```bash
   359→bd update bd-42 --status in_progress --json
   360→bd update bd-42 --priority 1 --json
   361→```
   362→
   363→**Complete work:**
   364→
   365→```bash
   366→bd close bd-42 --reason "Completed" --json
   367→```
   368→
   369→### Issue Types
   370→
   371→- `bug` - Something broken
   372→- `feature` - New functionality
   373→- `task` - Work item (tests, docs, refactoring)
   374→- `epic` - Large feature with subtasks
   375→- `chore` - Maintenance (dependencies, tooling)
   376→
   377→### Priorities
   378→
   379→- `0` - Critical (security, data loss, broken builds)
   380→- `1` - High (major features, important bugs)
   381→- `2` - Medium (default, nice-to-have)
   382→- `3` - Low (polish, optimization)
   383→- `4` - Backlog (future ideas)
   384→
   385→### Workflow for AI Agents
   386→
   387→1. **Check ready work**: `bd ready` shows unblocked issues
   388→2. **Claim your task**: `bd update <id> --status in_progress`
   389→3. **Work on it**: Implement, test, document
   390→4. **Discover new work?** Create linked issue:
   391→   - `bd create "Found bug" --description="Details about what was found" -p 1 --deps discovered-from:<parent-id>`
   392→5. **Complete**: `bd close <id> --reason "Done"`
   393→
   394→### Auto-Sync
   395→
   396→bd automatically syncs with git:
   397→
   398→- Exports to `.beads/issues.jsonl` after changes (5s debounce)
   399→- Imports from JSONL when newer (e.g., after `git pull`)
   400→- No manual export/import needed!
   401→
   402→### Important Rules
   403→
   404→- ✅ Use bd for ALL task tracking
   405→- ✅ Always use `--json` flag for programmatic use
   406→- ✅ Link discovered work with `discovered-from` dependencies
   407→- ✅ Check `bd ready` before asking "what should I work on?"
   408→- ❌ Do NOT create markdown TODO lists
   409→- ❌ Do NOT use external issue trackers
   410→- ❌ Do NOT duplicate tracking systems
   411→
   412→For more details, see README.md and docs/QUICKSTART.md.
   413→
